1. Statistics With jamovi
Welcome! This book is designed to help you learn statistics in a way that is practical, approachable, and relevant to real-world research and decision making.

title: Stats with jamovi
text:
What You’ll Learn
By using this book, you will learn how to:

Understand and interpret statistical results
Analyze data using jamovi
Connect statistics to research and real-world decisions
Communicate findings clearly using APA-style reporting

title: Stats with jamovi
text:
Get Started with jamovi
This book uses jamovi, a free and user-friendly statistical software.

Download jamovi: https://www.jamovi.org
Use the example datasets provided in each chapter
Follow step-by-step instructions to run analyses

You do not need prior experience with statistics or software—this book will guide you.

title: Stats with jamovi
text:
A Note About Learning Statistics
Statistics can feel overwhelming at first—and that’s completely normal.
This book is designed to help you build confidence step by step, even if you’ve struggled with math in the past.
Focus on understanding what results mean, not just how to calculate them.

title: Stats with jamovi
text:
About this Book
This is an open educational resource (OER), meaning it is free to use and continuously improved based on feedback from students and instructors.
This book was created to:

Make statistics more accessible and applied
Emphasize interpretation over memorization
Integrate hands-on data analysis using jamovi

It is built using open-source tools and updated regularly to reflect new features, examples, and feedback.
If you notice an error or have a suggestion, please email me at linnellda@uwstout.edu.

title: Stats with jamovi
text:
About the Author
Dana Linnell is a faculty member at the University of Wisconsin–Stout who teaches research methods and statistics. This book reflects her commitment to making statistics more accessible, applied, and meaningful for students.
Dedication: This book is dedicated to my graduate statistics professor, Dr. Dale Berger, who gave us a similar set of resources when he taught statistics at Claremont Graduate University. I still have my binder of handouts, homework assignments, and notes, which have been instrumental throughout my career. Thank you for showing me the joy of statistics!



Image of Dale Berger and Dana Linnell at her master’s graduation ceremony

title: 1.1 How to Use This Book
text:
What This Book Is Trying to Help You Do
This book is designed to help you learn statistics with jamovi, a free statistical software program. My goal is not for you to memorize formulas or become a mathematician. My goal is for you to learn how to use statistics to answer research questions, interpret results, and explain what those results mean.
If you are feeling nervous about statistics, you are not alone. Many students come into this course feeling unsure, anxious, or convinced that they are “not a stats person.” That is okay. Statistics is a skill, and skills develop with practice, feedback, and patience.
This chapter explains how to use the book, what the book assumes, how to navigate the online version, and how to get help when you need it.
By the end of this book, you should be better able to:

title: 1.1 How to Use This Book
text:
understand why a particular statistical test fits a research question
use jamovi to run statistical analyses
read statistical output without getting overwhelmed
interpret results in plain language
write up results clearly and accurately
make thoughtful decisions about data, assumptions, and evidence

title: 1.1 How to Use This Book
section: Common Challenges (and How to Handle Them)
text:
Common Challenges (and How to Handle Them)
“I don’t understand the math.”
That is okay. There is some math in this book because formulas can sometimes help us understand what a statistic is doing. However, this is not a course where the main goal is hand calculations. Most of the time, I care much more about whether you understand what the statistic means, why you chose it, and how to interpret the results.
“I got lost in jamovi.”
This happens, especially when you are new to statistical software. Slow down, go back to the previous step, and compare your screen to the example. A lot of learning jamovi is simply repetition. The more you practice opening data, checking variables, selecting analyses, and reading output, the more familiar it will become.
“I don’t know what the output means.”
This is one of the most common challenges in statistics. When you look at output, start with the research question. Ask yourself: What was I trying to find out? What test did I run? Which part of the output helps me answer the question? Then use the interpretation sections in this book to help connect the statistical results back to the research question.

title: 1.1 How to Use This Book
text:
Navigating This Book
This book is meant to be used as an online resource, not just read straight through from beginning to end. You will likely move back and forth between chapters, especially once you start working on assignments in jamovi.
A few features may help:

Use the sidebar/table of contents to jump between chapters and sections.
Use the search feature when you are trying to find a specific term, test, or jamovi step.
Use the previous/next buttons when you are working through chapters in order.
Adjust the font size or display settings if that makes the book easier to read.

My recommendation is to keep this book open while you work in jamovi. When you get stuck, search the book first, then go back to the relevant section and compare your screen to the example.

title: 1.1 How to Use This Book
text:
Technology Tips
You will need access to a computer that can run jamovi and open common file types such as Word, Excel, PDF, and data files.
I strongly recommend using the desktop version of jamovi when possible. jamovi Cloud exists, but the free version may not be sufficient for everything you need to do. The desktop version is free and is the version this book assumes you are using unless otherwise noted.
You will often be moving back and forth between this book, assignment instructions, datasets, and jamovi. If you have two monitors, this is a great course to use them. If not, learn how to snap windows side-by-side or switch quickly between open applications. On Windows, Alt+Tab lets you move between open applications. On Mac, use Cmd+Tab.
One very practical tip: close what you are not using. When you are working on statistics, try to have only the book, the assignment, your data file, and jamovi open. It reduces visual clutter, helps your computer run better, and makes it easier to focus.
Also, restart your computer regularly. I know this sounds like basic technology advice, but many mysterious software problems are solved by saving your work, closing programs, restarting your computer, and trying again. And sometimes that breather can help you better solve problems!

title: 1.1 How to Use This Book
text:
Getting Help
Learning statistics is not something most people do perfectly the first time. Getting stuck is part of the process.
If you are in one of my courses, check Canvas for the current options for getting help. Depending on the semester, this may include student hours, Teams, email, class discussion spaces, or support from a teaching assistant or graduate assistant.
When you ask for help, it is useful to include:

the chapter or assignment you are working on
what step you got stuck on
what you already tried
a screenshot of what you are seeing in jamovi, if relevant
your jamovi file, if you are asking about an analysis

You do not need to have a perfectly formed question before asking for help. “I got lost around this step” is a completely reasonable place to start.

title: 1.1 How to Use This Book
text:
A Final Tip
One final thing to keep in mind: most mistakes in statistics are not signs that you “can’t do stats.” They are usually small process errors: the wrong variable type, the wrong box in jamovi, a missed assumption check, or an interpretation that moved too quickly.
Slow down. Check your setup. Ask what the research question is. Then ask what the statistic is helping you decide. That process will take you much further than memorizing isolated rules.

title: 1.1 How to Use This Book
text:
Errors, Mistakes, and Suggestions
I am human, therefore I err.
If you find an error in this book, something that seems unclear, or something that does not match what you are seeing in jamovi, please let me know. It is especially helpful if you tell me the chapter or section, what seems wrong or confusing, and what you think might need to be updated.
I also welcome suggestions. This book is meant to be useful to students in this course and to anyone else learning statistics with jamovi. If something would make the book clearer, more accessible, or easier to use, I would genuinely like to know.

title: 2. Statistics Foundations
text:
What You’ll Learn
This chapter introduces some of the statistical language we will use throughout the book. You do not need to memorize every term right now. Instead, focus on understanding the big ideas: what data are, what kinds of variables we work with, how we describe data, and how we use samples to make inferences about populations.
You will come back to these ideas again and again. If something does not fully click the first time through, that is okay. The goal here is to build a foundation we can keep returning to.
As you read, pay special attention to the examples. In statistics, the same word can mean different things depending on how something is measured. For example, “time” might be categorical in one study and continuous in another. That is why I want you to focus less on memorizing definitions and more on asking: What does this variable actually represent?
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

title: 2. Statistics Foundations
text:
explain how data are used to answer research questions
recognize how datasets are organized into rows and columns
distinguish among nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio variables
explain the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics
use key statistical terms that will appear throughout the textbook

title: 2. Statistics Foundations
text:
Chapter Sections

2.1 Describing Data: Introduces what data are, how we summarize them, and why descriptive statistics matter.
2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement: Explains different types of variables and how they influence the analyses we choose.
2.3 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics: Builds the conceptual foundation for hypothesis testing and statistical inference.
2.4 Understanding Distributions and Variability: Explains how distributions are shaped and why variability matters.
2.5 Putting It All Together: Reviews major chapter concepts and helps connect them to future analyses.

title: 2. Statistics Foundations
text:
Why This Matters
These ideas matter because they help you avoid some of the most common mistakes in statistics.
For example, before you can run a statistical test, you need to know what kind of variables you have. Before you interpret a result, you need to know whether you are describing your sample or making an inference about a population. Before you report a mean, you need to know whether a mean makes sense for that variable.
These may seem like small details, but they shape almost every decision you make when analyzing data.

title: 2.1 Describing Data
text:
What Are Data?
Before we can analyze data, we need to understand what our data represent.
At its core, statistics is about using data to answer questions. In most research settings, data are organized in a dataset, often in a spreadsheet format. Each row usually represents one observation, such as one participant, and each column represents one variable, such as age, condition, or test score.
This structure matters because statistical software, including jamovi, expects data to be organized in a clear and consistent way.
First, let’s understand some basic statistics related to how we describe our data, including measures of central tendency (averages), measures of dispersion (spread), and measures of shape of the distribution (particularly a normal distribution). Here’s a video walking you through what we learn in this chapter.
In research, data are typically organized in a dataset (often a spreadsheet format), where:
Each value in the dataset tells us something about a specific observation on a specific variable.
::: {callout-info data-latex=““} Is “data” singular or plural?
In formal scientific writing, including APA style, *data* is usually treated as plural: “The data are consistent with the hypothesis.” In everyday speech, many people use *data* as singular: “The data is stored in a spreadsheet.”

You will see both. In your formal research writing, use the plural form unless your instructor or style guide says otherwise. :::

title: 2.1 Describing Data
text:
Rows represent individual observations (e.g., participants)
Columns represent variables (e.g., age, test score, group membership)

title: 2.1 Describing Data
text:
Data Need Context
Data are only meaningful when we understand what they represent. For example, a value of 75 does not mean much by itself. It could be a test score, heart rate, temperature, percentile rank, or something else entirely. To interpret data correctly, we need to know what each variable represents, how it was measured, and what the values mean.
This is why clear variable names and documentation are important when working with datasets.

title: 2.1 Describing Data
section: How to Read and Work With Data
text:
How to Read and Work With Data
To understand and use a dataset effectively, it’s important to recognize how data are organized. Well-structured datasets follow a few key principles:

Rows represent observations (e.g., one participant, one trial, or one case)
Columns represent variables (e.g., age, condition, score)
Each cell contains a single value for one observation on one variable
Variable names are clear and consistent, so you know what each column represents
Missing data are clearly labeled (e.g., NA), rather than left blank, although jamovi knows to treat blank cells as missing data
Data are stored in a clean, rectangular format, making them easier to analyze

Understanding this structure will help you read datasets, identify variables, and prepare your data for analysis in tools like jamovi.
Following these principles also helps prevent errors and makes data easier to analyze, share, and reproduce (Broman & Woo, 2018).

title: 2.1 Describing Data
text:
What Is a Variable?
A variable is anything we measure or record that can vary across individuals or observations.
Examples of variables include:

Age
Gender
Test scores
Survey responses

Some variables describe characteristics of participants, while others represent outcomes we are interested in understanding.
You will learn more about different types of variables in the next section.

title: 2.1 Describing Data
text:
Types of Data
Not all variables are the same. Broadly, variables fall into two categories:

Categorical variables describe groups or categories (e.g., gender, major, treatment condition)
Continuous/numerical variables represent quantities or amounts (e.g., age, height, test scores)

Variables are sometimes coded numerically (e.g., 0 = no, 1 = yes), but these numbers are simply labels. Coding a variable with numbers does not make it a continuous variable.
Understanding the type of data you are working with will help you decide how to summarize and analyze it. In the next section, we will look more closely at different types of variables and what they allow us to do in statistical analysis.

title: 2.1 Describing Data
text:
Why Do We Describe Data?
Once we collect data, the first step is to describe what we have. Raw data (a spreadsheet full of numbers) can be difficult to interpret on their own. Descriptive statistics help us:

Summarize large amounts of data
Identify patterns or trends
Understand what is typical or unusual
Prepare for further analysis

title: 2.1 Describing Data
section: What Does It Mean to Describe Data?
text:
What Does It Mean to Describe Data?
When we describe data, we are typically trying to understand three key features:

Center (What is typical?): This tells us what a “typical” value looks like, often summarized using measures like the mean or median. These are called measures of central tendency.
Variability (How spread out are the values?): This tells us how much the data differ from one another. Some datasets are tightly clustered, while others are more spread out. These are called measures of dispersion.
Shape (What does the distribution look like?): This refers to how the data are distributed—for example, whether values are evenly spread out or skewed in one direction.

You will learn how to calculate and interpret these in later chapters.

title: 2.1 Describing Data
text:
Types of Descriptive Information
There are two main ways we describe data:

Numerical summaries, using measures of central tendency (e.g., mean, median, mode) and dispersion (e.g., standard deviation, variance).
Visual summaries, using graphs and charts (e.g., histograms, bar charts) which help us quickly see patterns and differences.

title: 2.1 Describing Data
text:
Looking Ahead
Describing data is always the first step in any statistical analysis.
You will use these ideas when you:

Summarize your data (Chapter 4)
Visualize patterns (Chapter 5)
Begin hypothesis testing (Chapter 7)
Interpret results from statistical tests (Chapters 11–14)

Understanding your data before analyzing it will help you make better decisions and avoid mistakes.

title: 2.1 Describing Data
text:
Key Takeaways

Data are organized into rows (observations) and columns (variables)
Variables are characteristics or outcomes that can vary
Describing data involves understanding center, variability, and shape
Descriptive statistics summarize and simplify data
Describing data is the first step in any analysis

::: {callout-tip title=“Check Your Understanding”} By the end of this section, you should be able to:

In a dataset, what does each row represent? What does each column represent?

Give an example of a categorical variable and a continuous variable.

Why is it important that each cell contains only one value?

What are two features of a well-organized dataset?

Why do data need context to be meaningful? :::







TipAnswers






Rows represent observations (e.g., participants), and columns represent variables.

Categorical: major. Continuous: age.

title: 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement
text:
Continuous (Numerical) Variables: Interval and Ratio
In Section 2.1, we introduced two broad types of variables: categorical and continuous. We can refine this further into four levels of measurement. These levels help us understand what the values represent and what kinds of summaries and analyses are appropriate.
Understanding the type of variable you are working with will help you decide:
## Categorical Variables: Nominal and Ordinal
Categorical variables describe groups or categories. There are two main types of categorical variables: nominal and ordinal variables.
Nominal variables represent categories with no inherent order. There is a special type of nominal variable with only two categories (e.g., yes/no, present/absent), which we call a dichotomous variable.
Examples:
With nominal variables, we can:
We cannot meaningfully order or average these values.
Ordinal variables represent categories with a meaningful order, but the differences between values are not necessarily equal.
Examples:
With ordinal variables, we can:
However, we should be cautious about treating the differences between values as equal. Also, keep in mind that assigning numbers to categories (e.g., 1 = strongly disagree, 5 = strongly agree) does not automatically make a variable continuous. Always consider what the values represent.
Continuous variables, also called numerical variables, represent quantities or amounts. There are two main types of numerical variables: interval and ratio variables.
Interval variables have equal spacing between values, but no true zero point.
This means that differences between values are meaningful, but zero does not represent the absence of the construct.
Examples:

However, we should not interpret ratios (e.g., a score of 120 is not “twice as much” as a score of 60).
Ratio variables have equal spacing between values and a true zero point, meaning that zero represents the absence of the measured quantity.

title: 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement
text:
Temperature (°C or °F)
Standardized test scores (e.g., IQ scores)
Some psychological scale scores where zero is arbitrary



Compare differences between values (e.g., the difference between 90 and 100 is the same as between 100 and 110)
Calculate means and standard deviations
Create graphs and use many common statistical analyses





Reaction time (milliseconds)
Number of correct responses on a task
Time spent studying (minutes or hours)
Frequency of a behavior (e.g., number of times a behavior occurs)



Compute means and standard deviations
Compare differences between values
Make meaningful ratio statements (e.g., twice as much, half as much)
Use a full range of statistical analyses



Discrete vs Count Variables
Some continuous variables are measured on a scale (e.g., height, reaction time), while others are counts (e.g., number of correct responses).
More specifically:

Discrete variables take on countable values (e.g., number of children, number of errors)
Continuous variables can take on any value within a range (e.g., time, height, weight)

In practice, statistical software like jamovi typically treats both types as continuous variables, and we will do the same in this course.
crumbs: 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement

title: 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement
section: How Variable Type Affects What You Can Do
text:
How Variable Type Affects What You Can Do
Different types of variables allow for different kinds of summaries:

Nominal (categorical): counts and percentages
Ordinal: rankings, medians
Interval and ratio (numerical): means, standard deviations, and more advanced analyses

This distinction will become especially important when we begin summarizing data and selecting statistical tests.
crumbs: 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement

title: 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement
section: A Simple Way to Identify Variable Type
text:
A Simple Way to Identify Variable Type
When you encounter a variable, ask:

Is this describing categories or numbers?
If categories, is there a meaningful order? If so, then it’s ordinal; if not, then it’s nominal.
If numbers, does zero represent “none”? If so, then it’s ratio; if not, then it’s interval.

These questions can help you quickly determine the type of variable you are working with.
crumbs: 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement

title: 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement
section: The Same Construct Can Be Measured in Different Ways
text:
The Same Construct Can Be Measured in Different Ways
If levels of measurement still feel confusing, this is one of the most important things to remember:

A variable’s level of measurement depends on how it is measured, not just what it is.

For example, one semester a student asked, “Isn’t time a continuous variable?” The answer is: it depends.

If time is measured as pre vs. post, it is a nominal variable with two categories.
If time is measured as month of the year, it is ordinal because the categories have a meaningful order.
If time is measured as response time, exact date, or time remaining, it is typically continuous.

So “time” is not automatically one type of variable. Its level of measurement depends on the way the researcher defines and records it.
Here is another example. The construct studying could also be measured in different ways.









Name
Study_Continuous
Study_Ordinal
Study_Nominal




Name (Character)
Hours studied per day

Studies every day?

Notice that the same general idea—studying—can be measured as:

continuous: hours studied per day
ordinal: amount of studying on an ordered scale
nominal: whether the person studies every day or not

This matters because different ways of measuring a variable preserve different amounts of information.
In general, moving from continuous → ordinal → nominal reduces information. For example, if we turn hours studied into “low,” “medium,” and “high,” we lose the exact differences between people. For that reason, researchers usually want to avoid collapsing variables into categories unless there is a strong reason to do so.
For example, a mean split or median split turns a continuous variable into categories such as “above average” and “below average.” This may seem convenient, but it removes information and can make analyses less precise.
At the same time, “more information” does not always mean “better.” The best level of measurement depends on your research question and how the construct is most meaningfully measured.
Finally, just because values are written as numbers does not make a variable continuous. If I code cat = 1, dog = 2, and frog = 3, that is still a nominal variable. The numbers are just labels.
crumbs: 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement

title: 2.2 Types of Variables and Levels of Measurement
text:
Why This Matters
Understanding variable types helps you:

Choose appropriate summaries and graphs
Avoid common mistakes (e.g., averaging categories)
Prepare for statistical analysis

We will build on this foundation in later chapters when we learn how to summarize and analyze data.
::: {callout-tip title=“Check Your Understanding”} 1. Is “favorite color” a nominal, ordinal, interval, or ratio variable? 2. Is a 1–5 satisfaction rating scale nominal or ordinal? Why? 3. What makes ratio variables different from interval variables? 4. Why can’t we calculate a meaningful average for nominal data? 5. Give one example of each type of variable (nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio). :::






TipAnswers






Nominal
Ordinal, because the responses are ordered but the spacing between values is not guaranteed to be equal
Ratio variables have a true zero point; interval variables do not
Because the categories do not have numerical meaning or order
Answers will vary (e.g., nominal: major; ordinal: class rank; interval: temperature °C; ratio: height)

title: 2.3 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
text:
Descriptive Statistics
There are two broad types of statistics that we use when working with data: descriptive statistics and inferential statistics.
Descriptive statistics are used to summarize, organize, and describe the data we have collected.
Common ways we describe data include:
Descriptive statistics focus only on the sample data in front of us.

title: 2.3 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
text:
Measures of central tendency (e.g., mean, median, mode)
Measures of dispersion (e.g., range, standard deviation, variance)
Shape of the distribution (e.g., skew, kurtosis)
Visualizations (e.g., tables, graphs)

title: 2.3 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
text:
Inferential Statistics
Inferential statistics are used to make conclusions about a population based on data collected from a sample.
Because we usually cannot collect data from an entire population (due to time, cost, or access), we collect data from a sample and use statistical methods to make inferences about the larger group.
A parameter describes a population (e.g., the true average test score of all students), while a statistic describes a sample (e.g., the average score of the students we measured). In research, we use statistics to estimate unknown population parameters.
Common inferential statistics include:

t-tests
ANOVA
correlation
chi-square tests
regression

These methods help us answer questions like: “Is this difference meaningful, or could it have occurred by chance?”

title: 2.3 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
text:
How They Work Together
Descriptive and inferential statistics are closely connected:

We use descriptive statistics to summarize our sample
We use inferential statistics to generalize from the sample to the population

In practice, we almost always use both, but not all studies involve inference. Some studies are purely descriptive, focusing only on accurately summarizing the data collected. However, even when conducting inferential statistics, we begin by describing our sample.

title: 2.3 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
text:
An example
This can feel abstract, so let’s walk through a simple example.
Imagine we are conducting a study to examine whether watching Schitt’s Creek (a very good show, but not helpful for studying) versus watching video lessons on studying techniques (useful, but perhaps less exciting) affects test performance in UW–Stout students.
Our population of interest is all UW–Stout students (approximately 10k students). However, it is not feasible to collect data from every student due to time, access, and logistical constraints.
Instead, we collect data from a sample. For simplicity, we use students from two sections of an introductory psychology course (about 80 students). This is a convenience sample, not a random sample, but it works for illustrating our concepts.
We randomly assign half of the students to watch Schitt’s Creek and the other half to watch study skills videos.
One week later, all students take an exam, and we record their test scores.

title: 2.3 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
text:
Step 1: Describing the Sample
First, we use descriptive statistics to summarize the data.
We might:

Create a histogram of test scores (perhaps separated by group)
Calculate the mean score for each group
Examine the standard deviation to understand variability
Check the range of scores

These summaries help us understand what the data look like.

title: 2.3 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
text:
Step 2: Making Inferences
Next, we ask our research question: Which group performed better on the exam? To answer this, we use inferential statistics.
We take our descriptive statistics (means, standard deviations, sample sizes) and use a statistical test (in this case, an independent samples t-test) to evaluate whether the difference between groups is meaningful.
Rather than calculating this by hand, we typically use statistical software like jamovi, which provides a test statistic, a p-value, and an effect size.
These results help us determine whether the observed difference in our sample likely reflects a real difference in the population.

title: 2.3 Descriptive vs. Inferential Statistics
text:
Why This Matters
Understanding the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics helps you:

Clearly describe your data
Ask meaningful research questions
Make appropriate conclusions about a population
Avoid overgeneralizing from limited data

We will build on these ideas throughout the rest of the course, especially when we begin conducting inferential tests.
::: {callout-tip title=“Check Your Understanding”} 1. You collect data from 50 UW–Stout students and calculate the mean GPA. Is this an example of descriptive or inferential statistics? Explain briefly. 2. You use those 50 students to test whether UW–Stout students have a higher GPA than the national average. Is this descriptive or inferential statistics? Explain briefly. 3. A researcher reports that 62% of participants preferred online classes. What type of statistic is this? 4. Why do we typically use both descriptive and inferential statistics in the same study? 5. A study includes only descriptive statistics and no inferential tests. Give one reason why that might be appropriate. :::






TipAnswers






Descriptive. You are summarizing the sample you collected.
Inferential. You are using the sample to make a claim about a larger population.
Descriptive. It summarizes the sample data.
Descriptive statistics summarize what the sample looks like, and inferential statistics help us decide what the sample may suggest about the population.
Answers will vary. For example, the goal of the study may simply be to describe a group or summarize observations without making broader population claims.

title: 2.4 Understanding Distributions and Variability
text:
What is a Distribution?
So far, we have discussed what data are, how variables are measured, and the difference between descriptive and inferential statistics. Before we begin calculating statistics, it is important to understand what data look like as a whole.
When we collect data, we do not just have individual values; we have a distribution.
A distribution shows how values in a dataset are spread out and how often they occur.
Rather than focusing on a single value, distributions help us see patterns across all observations.

title: 2.4 Understanding Distributions and Variability
text:
Key Features of a Distribution
When we examine a distribution, there are a few key features to consider:

Center: Where do most values fall?
Variability: How spread out are the values?
Shape: What pattern do the values form?
Outliers: Are there unusually high or low values?

These features (center, variability, shape, and outliers) are what we will later summarize using descriptive statistics such as the mean and standard deviation.

title: 2.4 Understanding Distributions and Variability
text:
Shape of a Distribution
Distributions can take different shapes, and these shapes help us understand the data.
One particular distribution we care a lot about in psychological research is the normal distribution, which is a specific type of symmetrical distribution where:

Most values cluster around the center
Fewer values appear as you move away from the center
The left and right sides are approximately equal

Many statistical methods assume that data are approximately normally distributed, which is why this shape is especially important.

Skewed Distributions
However, not all data are normally distributed. In a skewed distribution, one side of the distribution stretches out more than the other.

Right-skewed (positive skew): tail extends to the right
Left-skewed (negative skew): tail extends to the left

Skew affects how we interpret the center of the data and which summary statistics are most appropriate.


Kurtosis (Shape of the Tails)
Another aspect of shape is kurtosis, which describes how heavy or light the tails of a distribution are.

Some distributions have more extreme values (heavier tails)
Others have fewer extreme values (lighter tails)

For now, it is enough to recognize that distributions can differ not just in symmetry, but also in how extreme values appear.

title: 2.4 Understanding Distributions and Variability
text:
Variability of a Distribution
Variability refers to how much the values in a dataset differ from one another.
Two datasets can have the same center but very different variability.
We will later quantify variability using measures such as:

Variance
Standard deviation

For now, focus on the idea that variability tells us how consistent or spread out the data are.

title: 2.4 Understanding Distributions and Variability
text:
Outliers
An outlier is a value that is unusually far from the rest of the data. Outliers can:

Affect the shape of a distribution (e.g., creating skew)
Increase variability
Influence summary statistics like the mean (but, notably, not the median)

Because of their impact, it is important to identify and think carefully about outliers when analyzing data.
Important note: Not all outliers are errors. Some may represent meaningful or important observations, so it is important to examine them carefully before deciding how to handle them. This will be discussed later in Chapter 6 (Cleaning data).

title: 2.4 Understanding Distributions and Variability
text:
Why This Matters
Understanding distributions helps you:

Interpret summary statistics like the mean and standard deviation
Recognize patterns and unusual values
Choose appropriate analyses
Avoid misleading conclusions

In the next chapters, we will learn how to:

Describe distributions numerically (Chapter 4)
Visualize distributions using graphs (Chapter 5)
Prepare and clean data for analysis (Chapter 6)

title: 2.4 Understanding Distributions and Variability
text:
A Quick Preview
When working with data, you will often ask:

Where is the center of the data?
How spread out are the values?
What shape does the distribution have?
Are there any unusual values?

These questions form the foundation of descriptive statistics.
::: {callout-tip title=“Check Your Understanding”} 1. What is a distribution? 2. What are the four key features of a distribution? 3. What is a normal distribution? 4. How do outliers affect a dataset? 5. Why can two datasets with the same mean still be very different? :::






TipAnswers






A distribution shows how values are spread out and how often they occur
Center, variability, shape, and outliers
A symmetrical distribution where most values cluster around the center
They can affect shape, variability, and summary statistics
Because they may differ in variability or shape

title: 2.5 Putting It All Together
text:
Big Picture Summary
In this chapter, we introduced the foundational ideas needed to understand and work with data. These concepts will be used throughout the rest of the course and serve as a reference point as you learn new statistical techniques.

title: 2.5 Putting It All Together
text:
Data are values collected to answer questions
A dataset is made up of cases (observations) and variables
Variables can be categorical or continuous, and how a variable is measured affects what we can do with it
Descriptive statistics summarize the data we observe
Inferential statistics allow us to make conclusions about a population based on a sample
Data form distributions, which can be described by their center, variability, shape, and outliers

title: 2.5 Putting It All Together
text:
How These Concepts Connect
These ideas are not separate—they work together as part of a process. You can think of working with data as a sequence:

We begin by defining variables and collecting data
These data form a dataset
When we examine all values together, we see a distribution
We use descriptive statistics to summarize that distribution
We use inferential statistics to answer research questions about a population

Understanding how these pieces fit together will make the rest of the course much easier.

title: 2.5 Putting It All Together
text:

title: 2.5 Putting It All Together
text:
Key Ideas to Remember

A variable’s level of measurement depends on how it is measured, not just what it represents
Assigning numbers to categories does not make a variable continuous
Collapsing variables into categories can reduce information
Distributions provide a complete picture of the data, not just a single value
Both descriptive and inferential statistics are typically used together

title: 2.5 Putting It All Together
text:
Looking Ahead
In the next chapters, you will begin working directly with data:

Chapter 3: Getting started with jamovi
Chapter 4: Describing data using statistics
Chapter 5: Visualizing data
Chapter 6: Cleaning and preparing data

Each of these chapters will build directly on the concepts introduced here.
::: {callout-tip title=“Check Your Understanding”} 1. A dataset includes the following variables:
-   Major
-   Stress rating on a 1–5 scale
-   Hours of sleep last night

For each variable:

-   Identify whether it is categorical or continuous
-   State one appropriate way to describe it

Why is it important to correctly identify variable types before running analyses?
A researcher reports only the mean for a variable. What important information might still be missing?
Explain the difference between a statistic and a parameter.
A variable is coded as:

1 = cat
2 = dog
3 = frog

Why is this still not a continuous variable? :::







Tip







Major → categorical → frequencies or percentages

Stress rating → ordinal (categorical) → median or frequencies

Hours of sleep → continuous → mean and standard deviation

Variable type determines what descriptive statistics, graphs, and analyses are appropriate. If a variable is classified incorrectly, the results may be misleading.
Variability, shape, range, and possibly sample size. The mean alone does not fully describe the data.
A statistic describes a sample, while a parameter describes a population. We often use statistics to estimate parameters.
Because the numbers are only labels for categories; they do not represent meaningful numerical quantities.

title: 2.5 Putting It All Together
text:
Reflection
Think about a dataset you might collect in your own field:

What variables would you measure?
Would they be categorical or continuous?
What might the distribution of your data look like?
What would you want to describe or compare?

Taking a moment to think through these questions can help you connect these concepts to real-world data.

title: 3. Overview of jamovi
text:
How to Use This Chapter
In this chapter, we begin working with statistical software: jamovi.
The goal of this chapter is not to perform statistical analyses yet. Instead, we will focus on becoming familiar with jamovi, understanding how data are organized in the software, and learning some basic tasks that will prepare us for later chapters.
The concepts introduced in Chapter 2—such as variables, datasets, and levels of measurement—are directly applied here. As you work through this chapter, you will start to see how those ideas appear in practice.
This chapter is organized into short pages that help you install jamovi, open data, navigate the interface, and check whether variables are set up correctly.
You do not need to memorize every menu option right away. For now, focus on becoming comfortable enough with jamovi that you know where to look and what to check before running an analysis.

title: 3. Overview of jamovi
text:
What You’ll Learn
By working through this chapter, you will learn how to:

Explain why this textbook uses jamovi
Install jamovi or identify where to get installation help
Open existing datasets, including Excel and SPSS files
Enter data manually when needed
Navigate the jamovi interface
Check variable names, descriptions, measure types, data types, missing values, and levels

title: 3. Overview of jamovi
text:
Chapter Sections

3.1 What Is jamovi?
3.2 Installing jamovi
3.3 Opening Data in jamovi
3.4 Navigating the jamovi Interface

title: 3.1 What Is jamovi?
text:
Why are we using jamovi?
jamovi is a free, open-source statistical software program that allows you to:
There are several reasons we use jamovi in this course:
And if you don’t want to hear it from me, here’s another person’s description of what jamovi is and why it’s a great alternative to SPSS that provides a useful path toward learning R.

title: 3.1 What Is jamovi?
text:
It’s free.: You will never have to pay to use jamovi. This means you can continue using it in future courses, research projects, or your career. There is an optional paid version of jamovi Cloud, but it is not required.
It’s open source.: jamovi is developed and maintained by the statistical community. As the developers describe it, “jamovi is made by the scientific community, for the scientific community.” This means the software is continuously improving and grounded in current best practices.
It’s built on R.: jamovi is built on top of the R statistical programming language. If you are interested in learning to code, jamovi provides a natural entry point. Many researchers (including me) use R in environments like RStudio for more advanced analyses. In fact, this textbook was created using RStudio and hosted on GitHub.
It’s easy to learn and use.: Compared to other statistical software (e.g., SPSS), jamovi is much more intuitive. Students typically find it easier to learn and prefer using it for their analyses.
It supports reproducibility.: jamovi saves your data, analyses, settings, and results all in one file. This makes it easy to return to your work, check what you did, and reproduce your results—an important part of good scientific practice.

title: 3.1 What Is jamovi?
text:
Additional jamovi videos and resources
Although this textbook aims to serve all your basic jamovi and statistical needs, here are some additional resources on jamovi that I recommend you bookmark and use when you are struggling or need additional support:

jamovi 2022 tutorials by Alexander Swan on YouTube
jamovi docs provide more support for using jamovi, including tips for transitioning from SPSS to jamovi and from jamovi to R
Introduction to jamovi LinkedIn Learning course by Barton Poulson, founder of datalab.cc (you can find the video on YouTube and other places now, too, if you do not have LinkedIn Learning) [Caution: this course uses an older version of jamovi]
practice resources and datasets for additional hands-on work by Neumann, Hood, & Neumann (2013)!

Keep in mind that some tutorials may use older versions of jamovi, so the menus or layout may look slightly different from what you see on your screen.

title: 3.2 Installing jamovi
text:
To install jamovi, go to the download page.
I recommend downloading the current version with the latest features, but you may want to get the solid version if you prefer something more stable.
jamovi has more documentation on how to install jamovi per operating system. Installation on macOS and Windows should be pretty straightforward.
A few notes:

Here’s some guidance if you are getting an error on Windows that “The app you’re trying to install isn’t a Microsoft verified app.”
Chromebooks can be trickier. If your Chromebook does not use Linux, you may have difficulty installing jamovi. If so, look for Chromebook-specific installation support, such as this video on installing jamovi on a Chromebook.

If you are unable to install jamovi right away, do not panic. Contact the instructor or teaching assistant for support. The most important thing is to start getting familiar with the software and how it is organized.

title: 3.3 Opening Data in jamovi
text:
Opening SPSS (.sav) files in jamovi
Before we can analyze data, we need to get it into jamovi and make sure it is set up correctly.
You can open existing datasets in jamovi, including files such as Excel spreadsheets and SPSS files.
When you open a file, take a moment to look through the data and ask:
That quick first look can save you a lot of problems later.
Alexander Swan has a video on opening files in jamovi.
If you are working with SPSS files (with a .sav file extension), jamovi can usually open them directly. This can be especially useful if you are working with older datasets or files shared by others.
Alexander Swan has an additional video on opening .sav files in jamovi.

title: 3.3 Opening Data in jamovi
text:
Annotating output in jamovi
Sometimes you may need to submit output files from jamovi with annotations. This can be helpful for showing your work, identifying key results, or explaining how you interpreted an analysis.
If you have not done this before, it is worth reviewing a short tutorial by Alexander Swan on annotating output in jamovi.

title: 3.4 Navigating the jamovi Interface
text:
Understanding variable setup in jamovi
Once you open jamovi, you will see a spreadsheet-like layout. This is where your data will appear.
Like we discussed in Chapter 2:
This structure is important to keep in mind, because many problems in statistical analysis begin with incorrectly structured or incorrectly labeled data.
I recommend watching a short video by Alexander Swan on navigating the jamovi interface in jamovi so you can become familiar with the layout and the basic menus. Note that some demonstrations you find online may use a Mac and may include extra modules, so the screen may look a little different from yours.

title: 3.4 Navigating the jamovi Interface
text:
Variable Name
First, you should use an informative yet brief variable name. Refer back to Chapter 2 for tips on variable names from Broman & Woo (2018).


Variable Description
Second, you should provide a longer description of the variable. You could provide the full text of the item in the survey, more details on how the variable was measured, or anything else that can help you or others better understand the variable.


Measure type
One of the most important parts of working in jamovi is correctly identifying your variable types. In jamovi, variables are set as one of four measure types:

 Nominal: categories with no order
 Ordinal: categories with a meaningful order
 Continuous: numerical variables
 ID: an identifier variable that you would not usually analyze, such as participant ID



Data type
Additionally, variables can be one of three data types:

Integer, meaning the values are discrete whole numbers
Decimal, meaning the values are numbers with decimals
Text, meaning the values are alphanumeric, not just numeric

These levels of measurement and data types connect directly to the ideas from Chapter 2. A variable may be coded numerically (for example, 0 = no and 1 = yes), but that does not mean it is continuous. You still need to specify the correct variable type.


Missing values
You can specify which values are intended to be missing values. For example, -99 is commonly defined as a missing value in a dataset.


Levels
For categorical variables, you can specify that text that aligns with numeric values of a variable. For example, if you have a dataset in which pretest is coded as 1 and posttest is coded as 2, you can specify this in the Levels box.
There is a toggle you can select to retain unused levels in analyses if you want any level that has no data to always show up (e.g., in a graph).






TipCheck Your Understanding




A variable is coded as:

0 = No
1 = Yes

Should this variable be set as continuous, ordinal, or nominal in jamovi? Why?
You open a dataset and jamovi labels a variable as continuous, but it represents categories such as:

1 = Freshman
2 = Sophomore
3 = Junior
4 = Senior

What should you do?
In an independent samples t-test:

What type of variable should go in the dependent variable box?
What type of variable should go in the grouping variable box?

Why is jamovi’s automatic detection of variable type helpful, but not enough on its own?
A student says, “If jamovi lets me run the analysis, it must be the right one.” What is wrong with that reasoning?









TipAnswers






Nominal. The numbers are labels for categories, not continuous quantities.
Change the variable type to ordinal, because the categories have a meaningful order.

Dependent variable → continuous
Grouping variable → categorical (nominal or ordinal)

jamovi’s automatic detection is helpful because it saves time, but it can make mistakes. You still need to confirm that the variable type matches what the variable actually represents.
jamovi can help guide you, but it is not a substitute for statistical reasoning. A variable can be set incorrectly, and software cannot always determine whether the analysis truly matches the research question.

title: 3.4 Navigating the jamovi Interface
section: How jamovi helps you work with variables
text:
How jamovi helps you work with variables
Once you understand how variables are defined, it’s important to understand how jamovi helps you work with them.

jamovi will try to automatically determine variable types.

When you enter data or open a dataset, jamovi will guess the data type and measure type for you. This is incredibly helpful—until it’s wrong.
Always double-check your variables before running any analyses. Incorrect variable types are one of the most common sources of errors in statistical work.

The icons help guide your analysis choices.

Those small icons next to variables are not just decorative—they tell you what kind of variable you are working with and where it can be used.
For example:

A ruler icon indicates a continuous variable
A Venn icon indicates a nominal variable
A histogram icon indicates an ordinal variable

These icons also appear in analysis menus to guide you.

jamovi helps prevent (some) mistakes—but not all.

When you run analyses, jamovi will often indicate what types of variables belong in each box.
For example, in an independent samples t-test:

The dependent variable box shows a ruler icon → you should enter a continuous variable
The grouping variable box shows a Venn diagram or historgram → you should enter a nominal or ordinal variable

This is incredibly helpful—but you still need to understand why those choices matter. jamovi is a guide, not a substitute for statistical reasoning.






WarningMost Common Mistake



Running an analysis with incorrectly specified variables. Always check:

Variable type (nominal, ordinal, continuous)
Data type (numeric vs text)
Whether the variable fits the analysis

title: 3.4 Navigating the jamovi Interface
section: A first look at your data
text:
A first look at your data
After loading or entering your data, your next step is to quickly check that your dataset is ready for analysis.
At this stage, that does not mean running formal statistical tests. Instead, it means making sure your data are set up correctly.
As you look through your data, consider:

Are the variables named clearly?
Are the variable types correct?
Are there obvious data entry mistakes?
Are there missing values that aren’t coded as missing?
Are there values that seem impossible or unusual?

We will learn more about cleaning and preparing data in Chapter 6, but building this habit now will make your analyses much smoother.
If something goes wrong in your analysis later, the first place to check is your variable setup.






TipCheck Your Understanding




You see a value of 999 in a test score variable where scores should range from 0 to 100. What should you do before analyzing the data?
Why is it important to look through your dataset before running analyses?
You notice that a variable contains the values:

Yes
yes
Y

Why might this be a problem?
If something looks wrong in your analysis output later, what is one of the first things you should check?









TipAnswers






Investigate the value before analyzing the data. It may be a data entry error, a missing value code, or some other issue.
Looking through the dataset helps you catch mistakes, incorrect variable types, missing values, and unusual or impossible values before they affect your results.
jamovi may treat these as different categories, which would distort frequencies and percentages.
Check your variable setup and the raw data values first.

title: 4. Describing Data in jamovi
text:
Chapter Sections
In this chapter, we begin describing our data using statistical summaries.
In Chapter 2, we learned what descriptive statistics are and why they matter. In Chapter 3, we learned how to set up and navigate data in jamovi. Now, we will bring those together and begin using jamovi to describe our data.
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

text:
4.1 Describing continuous variables
4.2 Describing categorical variables
4.3 Describing a continuous variable by a categorical variable
4.4 Choosing the right descriptive statistics
4.5 Writing up descriptive statistics
4.6 Common mistakes
4.7 Looking ahead

title: 4.1 Describing Continuous Variables
text:
Running descriptive statistics in jamovi
We will start by describing continuous variables, such as test scores, age, or reaction time.
To generate descriptive statistics:
Once you do this, jamovi will automatically generate output.
In the options panel, you can select which statistics to display.
For most purposes, you should include:
You may also include:

title: 4.1 Describing Continuous Variables
text:
Click Exploration
Select Descriptives
Move your variable into the Variables box





Mean: the average value
Median: the middlemost value
Standard deviation: average spread around the mean
Variance: squared spread
Minimum and maximum: the lowest and highest values, respectively
N (sample size): the total number of observations



Skewness: the shape of the tail of the distribution
Kurtosis: the shape of the height of the distribution

title: 4.1 Describing Continuous Variables
text:
Interpreting the output
Once you run the analysis, jamovi will provide a table of descriptive statistics.
When interpreting a continuous variable, focus on:

Center such as mean or median
Variability such as standard deviation or variance
Range such as the highest and lowest values

You can ask yourself the following questions to understand the distribution of the data:

Are the mean and median similar, which suggests a more normal distribution?
Are the values tightly clustered or widely spread out?
Are there potential outliers?
Do the highest and lowest values make sense based on the dataset?

This information is often used to write-up results in APA Style. For example:

The average test score was 78.4 (SD = 10.2), with scores ranging from 52 to 95.

This tells us:

the typical score (mean)
how spread out scores are (SD)
the range of observed values

I recommend you watch this video by Alexander Swan on how to describe continuous data in jamovi.






TipCheck Your Understanding




You run descriptive statistics and get:

Mean = 75
Median = 60

What might this suggest about the distribution?
Why is it important to report both the mean and the standard deviation?
Two datasets both have a mean of 80. One has a standard deviation of 2 and the other has a standard deviation of 15. What does this tell you?
A variable has a minimum of 18 and a maximum of 19, but the standard deviation is very large. What would you want to check?
If the mean and median are very different, which measure of center might be more useful to report? Why?









TipAnswers






The distribution may be skewed. Because the mean is greater than the median, it may be right-skewed.
The mean describes the center of the data, while the standard deviation describes how spread out the values are. Both are needed to understand the distribution.
The two datasets have the same average, but the second dataset is much more variable.
You would want to check the raw data and output carefully, because those values may not be consistent. There could be an error or a misunderstanding in the output.
The median may be more useful, because it is less affected by skew and outliers.




:::

title: 4.2 Describing Categorical Variables
text:
Running frequencies in jamovi
Now let’s describe categorical variables, such as gender, major, or condition.
To describe categorical variables:

title: 4.2 Describing Categorical Variables
text:
Click Exploration
Select Descriptives
Move your variable into the Variables box
Select the Frequency tables check box

title: 4.2 Describing Categorical Variables
text:
Interpreting output
Example:



Category
Count
Percent




Yes
45
56.3%


No
35
43.8%



Interpretation:

56.3% of participants responded “Yes,” while 43.8% responded “No.”

Key reminder
For categorical variables:

We do not calculate means or standard deviations
We describe how often each category occurs
A statistics table will be provided, but as the variable is categorical it will only provide N and Missing, as the other statistics are used for continuous variables

I recommend you watch this video by Alexander Swan on how to describe categorical data in jamovi.






TipCheck Your Understanding




Why would it be inappropriate to calculate the mean for a nominal variable?
What statistics are typically most appropriate for categorical variables?
A variable has the categories:

Psychology
Business
Engineering

What would be a useful way to describe this variable?
A student codes major as:

1 = Psychology
2 = Business
3 = Engineering

They then calculate the mean and report that the “average major” was 2.1. What is the problem with this interpretation?









TipAnswers






Nominal variables represent categories, not numerical quantities, so averages are not meaningful.
Frequencies (counts) and percentages.
Report how many participants fall into each category and the percentage in each category.
The numbers are only codes for categories. They do not represent meaningful numerical values, so the mean is not interpretable.

title: 4.3 Describing a Continuous Variable by a Categorical Variable
text:
Running grouped descriptives
Often, we want to describe a continuous variable across groups.
Example:
In Descriptives:
crumbs: 4.3 Describing a Continuous Variable by a Categorical Variable

title: 4.3 Describing a Continuous Variable by a Categorical Variable
text:
Click Exploration
Select Descriptives
Place your continuous variable in the Variables box
Place your categorical variable in the Split by box
crumbs: 4.3 Describing a Continuous Variable by a Categorical Variable

title: 4.3 Describing a Continuous Variable by a Categorical Variable
text:
Interpreting output
jamovi will now provide descriptive statistics for each group separately.
This allows you to compare:

group means
group variability
sample sizes

For example:

Students in the intervention group scored higher (M = 82.3, SD = 8.5) than students in the control group (M = 75.6, SD = 11.2).


Descriptive statistics are not inferential statistics. We can describe differences—but we are not yet testing whether those differences are statistically significant. That comes later. Be careful that you write up descriptive statistics as simply describing the data and not making causal inferences.







TipCheck Your Understanding




You compare test scores between two groups and find:

Group A: M = 80, SD = 8
Group B: M = 75, SD = 10

Can you conclude that the difference is statistically significant? Why or why not?
What does using the Split by box in jamovi allow you to examine?
Why might it be useful to compare both the means and standard deviations across groups?
A researcher reports that Group A scored higher than Group B descriptively. What additional step would be needed to test whether that difference is statistically significant?









TipAnswers






No. These are descriptive statistics only. You would need an inferential test to evaluate statistical significance.
It allows you to describe a continuous variable separately across the levels of a categorical variable.
The means show differences in center, and the standard deviations show differences in variability.
Conduct an inferential test, such as an independent samples t-test, if appropriate.
crumbs: 4.3 Describing a Continuous Variable by a Categorical Variable

title: 4.4 Choosing the Right Descriptive Statistics
text:
Not all statistics are appropriate for all variables.
Here is a quick reminder from Chapter 2. The type of variable determines:

what statistics you can compute
how you interpret the results

This is why correctly setting your variable type in jamovi (Chapter 3) is so important.



Variable Type
What to Report




Continuous
Mean, median, SD, range


Ordinal
Median, frequencies


Nominal
Frequencies, percentages









TipCheck Your Understanding




Which descriptive statistics would be most appropriate for each of the following?

Favorite color
Class rank
Reaction time

Why might the median be preferred over the mean for some variables?
A variable is ordinal, but a researcher reports the mean. What question should you ask before deciding whether that is appropriate?
Why is the type of variable so important when deciding how to describe data?









TipAnswers







Favorite color → frequencies and percentages

Class rank → median or frequencies

Reaction time → mean, standard deviation, range

The median is less affected by skew and outliers.
Ask whether the variable is being treated as approximately continuous and whether that choice is conceptually justified.
Variable type determines which summaries are meaningful and appropriate.

title: 4.5 Writing Up Descriptive Statistics
text:
Basic format (continuous variable)
Being able to compute statistics is not enough—you also need to communicate them clearly.
Example:

title: 4.5 Writing Up Descriptive Statistics
text:
M = , SD =



Participants reported moderate stress levels (M = 3.45, SD = 0.82).

title: 4.5 Writing Up Descriptive Statistics
text:
Including range

Scores ranged from ___ to ___

title: 4.5 Writing Up Descriptive Statistics
text:
Group comparisons

Group A (M = , SD = ) scored higher/lower than Group B (M = , SD = ).

title: 4.5 Writing Up Descriptive Statistics
text:
Tips

Always include units (e.g., test scores, minutes, ratings)
Round consistently (typically 2 decimal places)
Be clear and concise

title: 4.5 Writing Up Descriptive Statistics
text:
Examples from the literature
In small examples, we might write-up our descriptive statistics into a paragraph1 (note: I also describe an independent t-test and a chi-square test of independence in this paragraph):

In examples with many variables, we might write-up our descriptive statistics into a table2:







TipCheck Your Understanding




Rewrite this more clearly:

“The average score was 78 and spread was 10.”

What important information is missing from this write-up?

“Participants in Group A scored higher than participants in Group B.”

Why is it important to include units or context when writing up descriptive statistics?
A student writes:

“The mean major was 2.3.”

What feedback would you give?









TipAnswers






Example: “Participants had an average score of 78 (SD = 10).”
The means, standard deviations, and likely sample sizes for each group are missing.
Numbers alone can be unclear. Context helps readers understand what the values represent.
Major is a categorical variable, so the mean is not meaningful. They should report frequencies or percentages instead.

title: 4.6 Common Mistakes
text:
Here are a few common mistakes to avoid:

Reporting means for categorical variables
Ignoring variability (only reporting the mean)
Misinterpreting skewed data
Forgetting to report sample size
Not checking your data before analysis







TipCheck Your Understanding




What is one problem with reporting only the mean for a continuous variable?
Why is it a mistake to report means for nominal variables?
If a variable is coded incorrectly in jamovi, how might that affect your descriptive statistics?
A researcher finds a very large standard deviation. What are two possible explanations?









TipAnswers






The mean alone does not show variability, skew, or possible outliers.
Nominal variables are categories, not quantities, so a mean is not interpretable.
jamovi may calculate inappropriate statistics or allow the wrong type of analysis.
The data may truly be very spread out, or there may be outliers, errors, or incorrect coding.

title: 4.7 Looking Ahead
text:
In this chapter, we focused on summarizing data numerically.
In the next chapter, we will learn how to visualize data using graphs, which can often reveal patterns that numbers alone cannot.
Together, descriptive statistics and visualizations provide a complete picture of your data.

title: 5. Visualizing Data in jamovi
text:
Chapter Sections
In Chapter 4, we described data using numerical summaries such as means and standard deviations. In this chapter, we will describe data using visualizations.
Graphs allow us to see patterns in our data that may not be obvious from numbers alone. They are an essential part of understanding and communicating statistical results.
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: - Choose an appropriate graph based on variable type
- Create visualizations in jamovi
- Interpret what a graph shows
- Identify common issues in graphs

title: 5.1 Why Visualize Data?
text:
Visualizations in practice
In Chapter 4, we described data using numbers such as means, standard deviations, and percentages. While these summaries are essential, they do not always tell the full story.
You have likely heard the phrase, “a picture is worth a thousand words.” This is especially true in statistics. A well-designed graph can often communicate patterns in data more quickly and clearly than a table of numbers or a written paragraph.
Visualizations can help you:
For example, two datasets can have the same mean and standard deviation but look very different when graphed. Without a visualization, those differences might go unnoticed.
Visualizations are not just helpful for your own understanding—they are also essential for communicating results to others.
In research and professional settings:
A single well-designed graph can:
Because of this, visualizations are commonly used alongside (or sometimes instead of) detailed numerical summaries.

title: 5.1 Why Visualize Data?
text:
Journal articles often have word or page limits
Reports need to be clear and efficient
Audiences may not have strong statistical backgrounds



summarize large amounts of information
highlight key findings
make results more accessible to a broader audience

title: 5.1 Why Visualize Data?
text:
Key takeaway
Descriptive statistics and visualizations work together:

Statistics provide precise numerical summaries
Visualizations provide an overall picture of the data

Using both allows you to better understand and communicate your results.

Remember: it is incredibly important to always visualize your data! You never know what descriptive statistics may be hiding.

Here’s a video walking through why it’s so important to look at your data.

title: 5.2 Choosing the Right Graph
text:
The type of graph you use depends on the type of variable(s) you are working with.



Variable Type
Graph




Continuous
Histogram, density plot, boxplot


Categorical
Bar chart


Continuous + categorical
Bar chart, line chart, boxplot


Continuous + continuous
Scatter plot, line chart



Choosing the correct graph is just as important as choosing the correct statistical test.
Want to learn more about choosing the right chart? Chart Chooser provides a useful tutorial for choosing charts based on your variable type and objective.
jamovi has some plots built into its platform, both under the Plots drop-down menu in the Descriptives analysis and as options for many of the inferential statistical analyses.
We’ll learn more about how to choose and conduct better data visualizations later, but for now here are some recommended visualizations depending on what you are trying to do. Note that we will do most of our visualizations in jamovi, but we may also learn how to visualize data via Excel. There are also excellent LinkedIn Learning courses on data visualization in Excel and other tools that I strongly recommend; they are free for you if you are a UW-Stout student.

title: 5.3 Visualizing a Single Continuous Variable
text:
Creating a histogram in jamovi
As described above, a histogram and/or density plot is one of the best ways to visualize a single continuous variable. Both are useful for seeing the overall distribution of your data and to help check for normality. Which should you use? I think they’re both pretty great, and in fact you can combine the two to have a histogram plot with a density overlay. I like this option best because it presents more information and better lets us see if the if the density curve looks normally distributed.
When interpreting a histogram or density plot, look for:
Histograms help you visually understand: - mean and median (center) - standard deviation (spread) - skewness (shape)

title: 5.3 Visualizing a Single Continuous Variable
text:
Go to Exploration → Descriptives
Move your variable into the Variables box
Under Plots, select Histogram and/or Density



Shape: Is the distribution symmetric or skewed?
Center: Where are most values located?
Variability: How spread out are the values?
Outliers: Are there unusually high or low values?

title: 5.4 Visualizing a Categorical Variable
text: For this you would choose the single option under Bar Plots: Bar plot. It will simply show the frequencies of a categorical variable.

title: 5.5 Visualizing a Continuous Variable Split by a Categorical Variable
text: There are three options under Box Plots: Box plot, Violin (which is really a density plot with its mirror image!), Data (which can be Jittered or Stacked; I prefer Jittered so you can see the density of data points really well), and Mean. Personally, I love checking all four boxes! This gives you the best of all of them: the distribution of your data with the Violin option, the quartiles and mean with the Box plot option, a visualization of all your data points using the Data option, which is really useful because the other two options can be hiding weird things in your data, and what the Mean is.
