title: 8. BEAN
text:
Why BEAN matters
In Chapter 7, we introduced the logic of hypothesis testing and walked through the 4-step process for conducting inferential statistics. As part of that process, we encountered several key ideas, including p-values, alpha levels, effect sizes, power, and sample size.
In that chapter, these concepts were introduced individually to help you understand what each one means. However, in practice, these ideas do not operate independently. Instead, they work together as part of a system.
One way to understand how these pieces fit together is through the concept of BEAN, which stands for:
Although we will not cover these components in that exact order, BEAN provides a useful framework for understanding how these ideas relate to one another.
When conducting or interpreting research, we are constantly making decisions:
These are not independent decisions. Changing one of these factors will influence the others.
For example:
These relationships are at the heart of BEAN.

title: 8. BEAN
text:
How large of an effect are we trying to detect?

How much error are we willing to tolerate?

How likely do we want to be to detect a real effect?

How much data do we need?




Larger effects are easier to detect than smaller effects

Increasing sample size makes it easier to detect effects

Using a stricter alpha level reduces false positives but makes it harder to detect effects

Low power increases the likelihood of missing real effects

title: 8. BEAN
section: A key idea: these pieces work together
text:
A key idea: these pieces work together
Rather than thinking of effect size, alpha, power, and sample size as separate topics, it is more useful to think of them as parts of a system.
In that system:

Effect size, alpha, and sample size all influence power

Power reflects how likely we are to detect a real effect if one exists

In other words:

BEAN helps us understand what determines our ability to detect effects.

This is why these concepts are often discussed together, especially when researchers are designing studies or evaluating results.

title: 8. BEAN
section: What you will learn in this chapter
text:
What you will learn in this chapter
In the sections that follow, we will examine each component of BEAN in more detail:

Effect size

Alpha and p-values

Power

Sample size

As you read each section, pay attention not only to what each concept means, but also how it connects to the others. The goal is not just to understand each part, but to understand how they work together.
At the end of the chapter, we will bring everything together to see how these components interact in practice.

title: 8.1 Effect Sizes
text:
Effect size as part of BEAN
In Chapter 7, we introduced effect sizes as a way to describe the magnitude of an effect. You learned that effect sizes help us move beyond simply asking whether an effect exists (statistical significance) to asking how large or meaningful that effect is.
In this section, we build on that foundation and place effect sizes within the broader BEAN framework.
Within BEAN, effect size plays a central role because it directly influences our ability to detect an effect—that is, statistical power.
This simple idea has important implications:
This means that effect size is not just something we interpret after the analysis—it is something that fundamentally shapes whether we are likely to detect an effect in the first place.

title: 8.1 Effect Sizes
text:
Larger effects are easier to detect than smaller effects.



Larger effect sizes → higher power (all else equal)

Smaller effect sizes → lower power (all else equal)

title: 8.1 Effect Sizes
text:
Common effect sizes
Different statistical tests use different effect size metrics:

Cohen’s d: differences between two groups. Another similar effect size is Hedge’s g, which we often use when we have smaller sample sizes.
r (correlation): strength of relationships
η² (eta-squared): proportion of variance explained (ANOVA). Variants of eta-saured include partial eta-squared and omega-squared.
φ (phi): associations between categorical variables. Carmer’s V is also used.

In Chapter 7, we used Cohen’s d to quantify the difference between two groups. These metrics allow us to standardize effects so that they can be compared across studies and contexts.






Note



If you want to learn more, check out this great journal article by Daniel Lakens or this chapter on effect sizes by Daniel Lakens.

title: 8.1 Effect Sizes
text:
Interpreting effect sizes (with caution)
You may have learned general guidelines such as:

Small effect: ~0.20

Medium effect: ~0.50

Large effect: ~0.80

These guidelines can be useful starting points, but they are not universal rules.
The meaning of an effect size depends on context:

What is typical in the research area?

What outcomes are being measured?

What would be considered meaningful in practice?


Effect sizes should always be interpreted in light of theory, prior research, and practical implications.







Note



As an example, this study in the field of education collected effect sizes of many education interventions to figure out benchmarks for small (<.05), medium (<.20), and large (>= .20) effect sizes based on existing data rather than poor quality heuristics.

title: 8.1 Effect Sizes
section: What makes an effect practically significant?
text:
What makes an effect practically significant?
P-values tell us about statistical significance, but they do not tell us whether an effect is meaningful. That is the role of effect sizes—but determining what counts as “meaningful” is not always straightforward.
Lakens provides a useful overview of ways researchers can determine which effect sizes are interesting in practice in his paper on (Sample Size Justification). The paper has far more detail, but essentially can be boiled into three important questions we must ask ourselves when thinking about effect sizes:

What effect size do we expect based on past research?

What effect size do we care about?

What effect size can we realistically detect given our design?

This process is critical when designing studies and interpreting results. It also directly connects to the other components of BEAN.

title: 8.1 Effect Sizes
text:
Connecting effect size to BEAN
Effect size is tightly connected to the other elements of BEAN:

Effect size ↔︎ Power
Larger effects are easier to detect and increase power
Effect size ↔︎ Sample size (N)
Smaller effects require larger samples to detect
Larger effects can be detected with smaller samples
Effect size ↔︎ Alpha
Stricter alpha levels make smaller effects harder to detect

These relationships highlight an important idea:

The effect size you care about helps determine the sample size you need and the power your study will have.

title: 8.1 Effect Sizes
text:
Small effects can still matter
A common misconception is that small effect sizes are unimportant. This is not necessarily true.
Small effects may still be meaningful when:

They accumulate over time

They affect large populations

They influence important outcomes


What counts as “large” or “important” depends on the context, not just a rule of thumb.

title: 8.1 Effect Sizes
text:
A practical takeaway
When designing or evaluating a study, it is not enough to ask:

“Is there a statistically significant effect?”

We should also ask:

How large is the effect?

Is this effect meaningful in context?

Was the study capable of detecting an effect of this size?

These questions connect directly to the broader BEAN framework and will become especially important when we discuss power more fully in the next section.






TipCheck Your Understanding




How does effect size influence statistical power?

Why are “small, medium, large” guidelines for effect sizes only rough estimates?

Give one reason why a small effect size might still be important.

How does effect size relate to sample size in detecting effects?










TipAnswers






Larger effect sizes increase power, while smaller effect sizes decrease power

Because the meaning of an effect depends on context, theory, and application

Small effects can accumulate over time or impact many people

Smaller effects require larger sample sizes to detect, while larger effects can be detected with smaller samples

title: 8.2 Alpha and p-values
text:
What are p-values?
In Chapter 7, we introduced p-values and alpha as part of hypothesis testing. In that context, p-values helped us determine whether a result was statistically significant.
In this section, we revisit these ideas and place them within the broader BEAN framework.
Whereas effect sizes tell us about practical significance, they do not tell us about statistical significance. That is what p-values are for: they tell us whether our results are statistically significant or how surprising they are.
The formal definition of a p-value is that it is the probability of observing data that is as extreme or more extreme than the data you have observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
There are two important things to keep in mind about this definition:
Another way we could think of the p-value is: assuming there is no difference (i.e., the null hypothesis is true), how surprising is our data?

title: 8.2 Alpha and p-values
text:
The p-value is about the probability of our data. It is not about the probability of our hypothesis.

The p-value is based on the assumption that the null hypothesis is true.







Note



In APA style, we report the exact p-values rounded to three decimal places. In your jamovi settings, you can set the statistical significant reporting to “3dp” to satisfy APA requirements.

title: 8.2 Alpha and p-values
text:
Common misconceptions about p-values
You may have heard of p-values before, including some bad things. The biggest problem with p-values is that they are misunderstood, even by researchers.
Common misconceptions about p-values (by Daniel Lakens):

A non-significant p-value means the null hypothesis is true ❌

A significant p-value means the null hypothesis is false ❌

A significant p-value means the effect is practically important ❌

A significant p-value means there is only a 5% chance of being wrong ❌

These are misconceptions because p-values only tell us about the probability of our data under the null hypothesis—not whether the null or alternative hypothesis is true.
We never truly know whether the null hypothesis is true, and we can always be making a Type I or Type II error.






Note



These misconceptions come from the great work done by Daniel Lakens. You can read his blog post on the topic or take a great online course about improving your statistical inferences. He also has a great article that “The Practical Alternative to the p Value Is the Correctly Used p Value.”

title: 8.2 Alpha and p-values
text:
Are p-values bad?






Note



Many criticisms of p-values reflect misunderstandings of what they are designed to do. They do not confirm hypotheses—they help evaluate how surprising data are under the null hypothesis.


Some researchers have argued that we should abandon p-values entirely. For example, the journal Basic and Applied Social Psychology banned their use.
However, as Lakens argues:

“The practical alternative to the p-value is the correctly used p-value.”

There is nothing inherently wrong with p-values. The problem is how they are often used and interpreted.






Note



The journal Basic and Applied Social Psychology banned the use of p-values, claiming that NHST is invalid. Instead, they want researchers to focus on effect sizes and appropriate power analyses. Although these are laudable attempts–I agree that researchers should have highly powered studies and focus more on practical significance over statistical significance–I agree more with researchers like Daniel Lakens who point out that the problem is researchers want NHST to do what it does not do. They want statistical procedures that can confirm a hypothesis, which NHST cannot do. While larger samples and a focus on effect sizes can help, we should trust NHST to do what it can do, which is to tell us about the probability of the surprisingness of our data if the null hypothesis is true. Read more about the journal’s statement here: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/01973533.2015.1012991

title: 8.2 Alpha and p-values
text:
Alpha: What counts as “surprising”?
The p-value tells us how surprising our data are—but how surprising is surprising enough?
That is where alpha (α) comes in. Alpha determines the critical region of significance—the threshold at which we decide to reject the null hypothesis.





Critical area of statistical significance




The alpha level represents the point at which we say:

“This result is so unlikely under the null hypothesis that we will reject it.”

title: 8.2 Alpha and p-values
text:
Why is alpha usually .05?
The .05 threshold comes from Fisher (1925), who suggested it as a convenient cutoff (roughly 1 in 20, or about 2 standard deviations from the mean). More specifically, he said:

A deviation exceeding the standard deviation occurs about once in three trials. Twice the standard deviation is exceeded only about once in 22 trials, thrice the standard deviation only once in 370 trials…. The value for which P = .05, or 1 in 20, is 1.96 or nearly 2 ; it is convenient to take this point as a limit in judging whether a deviation is to be considered significant or not. Deviations exceeding twice the standard deviation are thus formally regarded as significant.

However, this value is largely a historical convention, not a universal rule. Basically, .05 was convenient. It was 1/20. It was around 2 standard deviations from the mean in a normal distribution. For some reason, it caught on (maybe the “formally regarded as significant” was why).
Even Fisher later argued that researchers should think critically about alpha and, in some cases, use stricter thresholds (e.g., .01).






Note



If you want to read more, this is a short read on the history of the .05 alpha level: https://www2.psych.ubc.ca/~schaller/528Readings/CowlesDavis1982.pdf

title: 8.2 Alpha and p-values
text:
Choosing an alpha level
In this course, we typically use α = .05.
In practice, however, choosing alpha involves tradeoffs:

Lower alpha → fewer false positives (Type I errors)

Higher alpha → greater ability to detect effects (higher power)

If you are more concerned about false positives, you might lower alpha.
If you are more concerned about missing real effects, you might prioritize power.
Researchers should justify their alpha level rather than relying on defaults.






Note



What does this look like in practice? In a Psi Chi article by Kobza and Salter (2016), they stated, “Due to the high number of statistical tests conducted, a lower p value (.01) was used to determine significance.” (p. 75).



How do I know what alpha researchers are using?
Ideally, all researchers should be reporting their alpha values in all their publications. For example, in a Psi Chi article by Campero-Oliart and colleagues (2020), they stated, “Alpha levels for all analyses were set to .05.” (p. 47).
In reality, we typically don’t report anything and assume a default of .05 without explicitly stating so (and yes, I am also guilty of this). However, you should always be thinking critically of your alpha value and, if you are using something other than .05, you should explain what level you choose and why.

title: 8.2 Alpha and p-values
text:
Alpha and p-values within BEAN
Within BEAN, alpha and p-values interact with the other components in important ways:

Alpha ↔︎ Power: Lower alpha makes it harder to detect effects (reduces power)

Alpha ↔︎ Effect size: Smaller effects are harder to detect under stricter alpha levels

Alpha ↔︎ Sample size: Larger samples may be needed when using stricter alpha levels

Alpha is not just a threshold—it directly affects our ability to detect effects.

title: 8.2 Alpha and p-values
text:
A practical takeaway
When interpreting results, it is not enough to ask:

“Is p < .05?”

We should also ask:

How large is the effect?

How much data was collected?

Was the study sufficiently powered?

This is why p-values should always be interpreted alongside the other components of BEAN.

title: 8.2 Alpha and p-values
text:
Videos and resources
The following video walks through some of the effect sizes, alpha, and power stuff.









You might also like this chapter or video on p-values by Daniel Lakens.









Another video recommended by a student is this one from zedstatistics on p-values.















TipCheck Your Understanding




What does a p-value represent?

Why is it incorrect to say a p-value is the probability that the null hypothesis is true?

How does lowering alpha affect power?

Why can a small effect become statistically significant with a large sample size?

Why should p-values not be interpreted in isolation?









TipAnswers






The probability of observing the data (or more extreme) assuming the null hypothesis is true

Because p-values describe the probability of the data, not the hypothesis

It decreases power, making effects harder to detect

Because larger samples increase the ability to detect small effects

Because they depend on effect size, sample size, and alpha

title: 8.3 Power
text:
What is power?
In Chapter 7, we introduced power as part of hypothesis testing. In this section, we expand on that idea and connect power more explicitly to the BEAN framework.
Power is the probability that you will observe a statistically significant effect if there is a true effect. In other words, power is the probability of a statistically significant result assuming the alternative hypothesis is true.
Power can range from 0–100%, but typically people set it at 80%. This means that, if we set power at 80% and if the alternative hypothesis is true, then we have an 80% chance of detecting the effect.
However, in practice, power is often far lower than 80% due to insufficient sample sizes for the effect sizes of interest, something we’ll investigate in the final section of this chapter and in the homework.
Power is based on the assumption that the alternative hypothesis is true whereas the p-value is based on the assumption that the null hypothesis is true. If we want to increase the likelihood of supporting our alternative hypothesis, then we should be doing all we can to increase our power!

title: 8.3 Power
text:
Note



Just like with alpha, you have to critically set your power level too. Although we commonly set it to 80%, that still leaves a large likelihood of a false negative if the alternative hypothesis is true. To better detect a statistically significant result (assuming the alternative is true), we should aim for higher power levels.

title: 8.3 Power
text:
Power vs the p-value
Power is the probability that you will observe a statistically significant effect if there is a true effect. In other words, power is the probability of a statistically significant result assuming the alternative hypothesis is true.
Let’s compare this to our definition of the p-value: the probability of observing data that is as extreme or more extreme than the data you have observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
Although these definitions may sound similar, they are actually answering two very different questions:

The p-value asks: “If there is no effect (the null hypothesis is true), how surprising are these data?”
Power asks: “If there is a real effect (the alternative hypothesis is true), how likely are we to detect it?”

The key difference is the assumption each one makes:

p-values are calculated assuming the null hypothesis is true

Power is calculated assuming the alternative hypothesis is true


In this sense, they are two sides of the same coin—but they are not interchangeable.

Another way to think about it:

The p-value helps us evaluate evidence against the null hypothesis

Power tells us how likely we are to find evidence for the alternative hypothesis when it is true

This distinction is critical for understanding hypothesis testing. A small p-value tells us that our data are unlikely under the null hypothesis, but it does not tell us how likely we were to detect the effect in the first place—that is the role of power.

title: 8.3 Power
section: A key idea: power reflects detectability
text:
A key idea: power reflects detectability
Power is best understood as our ability to detect effects.

High power → we are likely to detect real effects

Low power → we are likely to miss real effects

This makes power a central concept in BEAN:

Effect size, alpha, and sample size all work together to determine power.

title: 8.3 Power
text:
B(E)A(N): Alpha and power
Let’s start putting all this together. Here’s all our definitions so far (remember, we can never truly know whether the null or alternative hypothesis is true):

Alpha is the value we set to determine what constitutes a statistically significant result, assuming the null hypothesis is true.

Power is the value we set at for what constitutes a statistically significant result, assuming the alternative hypothesis is true.

A Type I error is when we get a statistically significant result but in fact the null hypothesis is true.

A Type II error is when we do not get a statistically significant result but in fact the alternative hypothesis is true.

A correct inference is when we either

get a statistically significant result when the alternative hypothesis is true or

do not get a statistically significant result when the null hypothesis is true.








TipCheck Your Understanding



Consider the following table:









H0 is true
H1 is true




p < .05 (statistically significant)
A
B


p > .05 (statistically non-significant)
C
D



Using what you know about alpha, power, and errors, determine which cell(s) each of the following belong in:

Alpha

Power

Type I error

Type II error

Correct inference (there are two!)

Before checking the answers, try to reason through each one carefully.








TipAnswers






Alpha → A: Alpha is the probability of a statistically significant result when the null hypothesis is true (Type I error)
Power → B: Power is the probability of a statistically significant result when the alternative hypothesis is true
Type I error → A: Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true
Type II error → D: Failing to reject the null hypothesis when the alternative hypothesis is true
Correct inference → B and C

B: Detecting a real effect when it exists

C: Correctly concluding no effect when the null hypothesis is true

title: 8.3 Power
section: How alpha and power relate to one another
text:
How alpha and power relate to one another
We’ll eventually see how power, alpha, effect sizes, and sample size all interrelate (BEAN!). For now, let’s examine how alpha and power relate to one another in the context of hypothesis testing.
Note that a lot of this comes from Daniel Lakens’ Improving your Statistical Inference work.






Warning



Remember: we never know for certain if the null or alternative hypotheses are true! These are for illustrative purposes.



Assuming the null hypothesis is 100% true
If we set alpha at 5%, power at 80%, and assume the null hypothesis is 100% true (meaning the effect definitely does not exist), we could fill in the following table as such:









H0 is true
H1 is true




p < .05 (statistically significant)
5%
0%


p > .05 (statistically non-significant)
95%
0%



How did I get there? First, we’re assuming the null hypothesis is 100% true. Therefore, that column must add up to 100%. If the null hypothesis is 100%—and we know our hypotheses must be mutually exclusive—then the alternative hypothesis must be 0% true. Therefore, that column must add up to 0%. The whole table must equal to 100% to exhaust all options.
Therefore, our power doesn’t matter at all in this case. If the null is true, then it doesn’t matter what power we have to detect the alternative effect because the alternative effect does not exist. So we instead use alpha and put it in the upper left cell. Note that our alpha level is the Type I error rate we are setting!
If the whole table must equal to 100%, and the left column must equal to 100% because the null is 100% true, then 100%-5% = 95% for the correct inference. In other words, if we tested this effect (that doesn’t exist) 100 times, around 95% of the time we would get a non-significant p-value (p > .05) and about 5% of the time we would get a significant p-value and be making a Type I error.
We can visualize our p-value distribution using this handy interactive calculator. We set our effect size (ES) to be d = 0, meaning there is no effect (i.e., the null hypothesis is true). This results in a uniform distribution of p-values. Exactly 5% of p-values would fall between p = 0 and p = .05 (the shaded region to the right of the red dotted line). That aligns with our Type I error rate as well (5%). Go ahead and play around with the interactive calculator and try moving the slider for sample size! Notice that it does absolutely nothing. We’ll understand why when we put everything together.



Assuming the alternative hypothesis is 100% true
Let’s try out the opposite: assume the alternative hypothesis is 100% true, alpha is 5%, and power is 80%. What would you put in the table?









H0 is true
H1 is true




p < .05 (statistically significant)
0%
80%


p > .05 (statistically non-significant)
0%
20%



How did I get those numbers? First, remember that the table must equal to 100% (hypotheses must be exhaustive). Second, remember that the alternative hypothesis is 100% true so that column must equal to 100% (and because hypotheses must be mutually exclusive, the other column must equal to 0%).
Therefore, it doesn’t matter what we set alpha to. We cannot get a Type I error if the alternative hypothesis is true! We can only get a correct inference or make a Type II error.
We set power to 80%, and power is the probability of getting a statistically significant result assuming the alternative hypothesis is true. Therefore it goes in the top right cell. Notice that power is the probability of correctly detecting a statistically significant effect!
With simple arithmetic, 100%-80% = 20% is our Type II error (aka a false negative). If we were to test for this effect 100 times, about 80 times we would correctly detect the effect and about 20 times we would fail to detect the effect.
Let’s visualize this. Go back to our handy interactive calculator and put d = .8 as our effect size and n = 25 per group to our sample size. Notice now what our distribution of p-values looks like! Rather than a uniform distribution, now we have a steep exponential distribution. I have chosen to highlight all p-values in the range of p > .05, which in that selection is roughly 20% (our Type II error rate from above). The number of p-values < .05 is roughly 80%. Play again with the ES slider and sample size slider. Notice now that it makes a difference to our power! You’re getting a glimpse into how power depends on our alpha, effect size, and sample size. BEAN!



Assuming a 50/50 split on the null and alternative hypotheses
In reality, we never truly know whether the null or alternative hypotheses are true. Maybe we’re testing a new effect and we are completely 50/50 on whether the null or alternative hypothesis is true. Let’s keep our alpha and power the same (5% and 80%, respectively) and fill out our table now:









H0 is true
H1 is true




p < .05 (statistically significant)
2.5%
40%


p > .05 (statistically non-significant)
47.5%
10%



How did we get there? Again: the table must equal to 100% and we specified ahead of time that we thought it was about 50% true for each of the hypotheses, so each column must equal to 50%. 50% of 5% (alpha) is 2.5% and 50% of 80% (power) is 40%. We then fill out the bottom row based on arithmetic.
Imagine this were your study and you got a significant p-value. What could you conclude? Either you reject the null hypothesis or fail to reject the null hypothesis. But which one? In reality, we never know, but there are things we can do to increase the likelihood that our statistically significant result is because the alternative hypothesis is true and not the null hypothesis.
Right now, based on the values we have set (alpha = 5% and power = 80%), it is 16 times more likely that a statistically significant result means the alternative hypothesis is true than the null hypothesis is true. We calculate this with the first row of data: the probability of a statistically significant result if the alternative is true divided by the probability of a statistically significant result if the null is true: 40%/2.5% = 16.
You might be fine with a statistically significant result being 16 times more likely that the alternative is true compared to the null, but what can we do to increase this likelihood?

Increasing power






Note



Remember: the researcher sets the alpha and power levels! Let’s find out what happens when we adjust those values.


Let’s try it again, but this time let’s increase our power to 95% and keep our alpha at 5% (50/50 on the hypotheses). Fill out the table!









H0 is true
H1 is true




p < .05 (statistically significant)
2.5%
47.5%


p > .05 (statistically non-significant)
47.5%
2.5%



Now it is 19 times more likely (47.5/2.5 = 19) that the alternative hypothesis is true than the null hypothesis is true. Awesome! We have now discovered that increasing power increases the likelihood that a statistically significant result means our our alternative hypothesis is true.


Decreasing alpha
Let’s do another example in which we still have a 50/50 on the hypotheses but we reduce our alpha to 1% and keep our power at 80%. Fill out the table!









H0 is true
H1 is true




p < .05 (statistically significant)
.5%
40%


p > .05 (statistically non-significant)
49.5%
10%



Now it is 80 times more likely (40/.5 = 80) that the alternative hypothesis is true than the null hypothesis is true. Awesome!
We have now discovered that decreasing alpha, not increasing power, is the best way for increasing the likelihood that a statistically significant result means our our alternative hypothesis is true.


Your turn: Increasing power AND decreasing alpha






TipCheck Your Understanding



Let’s see what happens when we both increase power and decrease alpha.
Assume: - The null and alternative hypotheses are each 50% likely
- Alpha (α) = 1%
- Power = 95%
Fill out the table below:









H0 is true
H1 is true




p < .05 (statistically significant)
A
B


p > .05 (statistically non-significant)
C
D



Then answer:

What value goes in each cell (A, B, C, D)?

How much more likely is it that a statistically significant result reflects the alternative hypothesis rather than the null hypothesis?

Take a moment to work through this before checking your answer.








TipAnswers





Step 1: Fill in the table
Because each hypothesis is 50% likely:

Alpha = 1% → 50% of 1% = 0.5% → A

Power = 95% → 50% of 95% = 47.5% → B

Now fill in the remaining cells:

C = 50% − 0.5% = 49.5%

D = 50% − 47.5% = 2.5%

Final table:









H0 is true
H1 is true




p < .05
0.5%
47.5%


p > .05
49.5%
2.5%



Step 2: Compare likelihoods
We compare the probability of a significant result under each hypothesis:
[ = 95 ]
→ A statistically significant result is 95 times more likely to reflect the alternative hypothesis than the null hypothesis.
Interpretation:
By increasing power and decreasing alpha, we dramatically improve our confidence that a statistically significant result reflects a true effect rather than a false positive.
This illustrates how BEAN works:

Lower alpha → fewer false positives

Higher power → more true positives

Together, they make our results much more trustworthy.

title: 8.3 Power
text:
Connecting power to BEAN
Power is not an isolated concept—it is determined by:

Effect size → larger effects are easier to detect

Sample size (N) → more data increases power

Alpha → stricter thresholds reduce power


BEAN determines power, and power determines whether we detect effects.

title: 8.3 Power
text:
A practical takeaway
When evaluating or designing a study, we should ask:

Do we have enough power to detect the effect?

Is the effect size realistic?

Is our alpha appropriate?

Do we have enough data?

These questions help us understand whether a study is capable of detecting meaningful effects.

title: 8.3 Power
text:
Want another explanation of power?
If power still feels a bit abstract, the following video provides another walkthrough of the concept and may help reinforce how power relates to hypothesis testing, Type II error, alpha, and sample size.















TipCheck Your Understanding




What does statistical power represent?

How is power different from a p-value?

What happens to power when effect size increases?

What happens to power when sample size increases?

Why is power important for interpreting non-significant results?

Which change has the largest impact on confidence in significant results: increasing power or decreasing alpha?










TipAnswers






The probability of detecting a real effect when it exists

Power assumes the alternative is true; p-values assume the null is true

Power increases

Power increases

Because low power may lead to missing real effects

Decreasing alpha

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
What is sample size?
In the previous sections, we explored effect sizes, alpha, and power. In this section, we turn to sample size (N) and bring everything together through power analysis.
Sample size is the total number of participants in a study (often denoted as n). In between-subjects designs, we also consider how many participants are in each group, especially if group sizes are unequal.

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
section: How do we determine what sample size I need?
text:
How do we determine what sample size I need?
There are several ways researchers determine sample size:

Resource constraints: Time, budget, and access to participants may limit sample size
Accuracy or a priori power analysis: Based on desired power and expected effect size
Heuristics: Rules of thumb from prior literature (generally discouraged)







Note



This information comes from Daniel Lakens’ paper and chapter out on the topic and his sample size justification online tool.


The first option is more of a research methods issue and will not be discussed in this statistics textbook. The third option should be avoided whenever possible.
This leads us to the most important approach:

Conducting an a priori power analysis

We’ll cover it broadly here, but if you want to learn more about conducting power analyses in jamovi Bartlett & Charles (2022) have a great preprint on the topic.

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
BEAN: Power analysis
We previously saw how alpha and power relate to one another. Through interactive tools, you may have also noticed that effect size and sample size relate to alpha and power.
This is the core idea of BEAN:

If you know three components of BEAN, you can determine the fourth.

Power, effect size, alpha, and sample size all interrelate. Typically, we are trying to answer one of three questions:

What sample size do I need given effect size, alpha, and power?
What power do I have given effect size, alpha, and sample size?
What effect size can I detect given alpha, power, and sample size?

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
Tools for power analysis
There are several tools available for conducting power analyses:

G*Power is perhaps most widely used; there are various guides on how to use G*Power, including Erdfelder et al. (1996) and Faul et al., (2007)
Specialized calculators for specific analyses
Simulation-based approaches (more advanced)







Note



If you’d like to learn more about power analyses in a variety of experimental designs using G*Power or R packages, I recommend this article by Perugini et al. (2018) called “A practical primer to power analysis for simple experimental designs.”


For this course, we will use the PAMLj module in jamovi, which simplifies these calculations.

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
section: Using PAMLj for power analysis in jamovi
text:
Using PAMLj for power analysis in jamovi
Instead of jpower, we will use the PAMLj module in jamovi for power analysis. PAMLj provides a flexible and user-friendly way to conduct power analyses across a range of designs and statistical tests.

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
What PAMLj can do
PAMLj allows you to conduct power analyses by specifying different combinations of BEAN components.
You can use it to:

Calculate required sample size (N)
Estimate power for a given design
Determine what effect size is detectable

These directly correspond to the three core BEAN questions:

What sample size do I need?
What power do I have?
What effect size can I detect?


PAMLj operationalizes BEAN—it allows you to solve for one component given the others.

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
section: How to use PAMLj in jamovi
text:
How to use PAMLj in jamovi
To conduct a power analysis:

Open jamovi
Install and load the PAMLj module (from the Modules library)
Navigate to the power analysis section
Choose your statistical test (e.g., independent samples t-test)

Then, you will:

Select what you want to solve for (N, power, or effect size)
Enter the remaining BEAN components
Specify:

One-tailed vs two-tailed test
Group sizes (if applicable)

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
Applying PAMLj to BEAN questions

1. What sample size do I need?
This is the most common use of power analysis. To determine required sample size:

Choose Solve for N
Enter:

Expected effect size (from Section 8.1)
Desired power (e.g., 80% or higher)
Alpha (e.g., .05)


PAMLj will return the required sample size per group.
Interpretation:

Smaller effect size → larger required sample
Higher desired power → larger required sample
Lower alpha → larger required sample


Sample size is the lever we adjust to achieve sufficient power.



2. What power do I have?
Sometimes, sample size is fixed due to constraints. To determine power:

Choose Solve for power
Enter:

Sample size
Effect size
Alpha


PAMLj will estimate your power.
Interpretation:

Low power → high risk of missing real effects
High power → greater likelihood of detecting effects


This helps us evaluate whether a study is capable of detecting meaningful effects.



3. What effect size can I detect?
This question is especially useful when planning studies with limited resources. To determine detectable effect size:

Choose Solve for effect size
Enter:

Sample size
Desired power
Alpha


PAMLj will estimate the smallest effect size you can reliably detect.
Interpretation:

Small sample → only large effects detectable
Large sample → smaller effects detectable


This connects directly back to Section 8.1: what effect size do we care about?

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
Example: Applying BEAN with PAMLj
Let’s revisit our earlier example:

Effect size: d = .8
Alpha: .05
Sample size: 25 per group
Two-tailed test

Using PAMLj, we estimate:

Power ≈ 79%

This tells us:

We have about a 79% chance of detecting an effect of this size.

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
Try it yourself
Use PAMLj to explore:

What happens to sample size when you increase power?
What happens to power when you decrease effect size?
What happens when you lower alpha?

These explorations help you build intuition about how BEAN works in practice.






TipCheck Your Understanding




What are the three main questions PAMLj helps answer?
What happens to required sample size when desired power increases?
What happens to detectable effect size when sample size increases?
Why is power analysis considered the practical application of BEAN?









TipAnswers






Sample size, power, and detectable effect size
It increases
Smaller effects become detectable
Because it uses all BEAN components together to guide study design

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
section: Bringing BEAN together: Making research decisions
text:
Bringing BEAN together: Making research decisions
By this point, you have seen each component of BEAN individually:

Effect size → what we care about

Alpha → how strict we are about evidence

Power → our ability to detect effects

Sample size → how much data we collect

Power analysis is where all of these come together.

BEAN is not just a set of concepts—it is a system of tradeoffs.

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
There is no single “correct” choice
One of the most important takeaways from this chapter is that there is rarely a single correct answer when designing a study.
Instead, researchers must balance competing priorities:

Want to detect small effects? You need a larger sample

Want to reduce false positives? You may need a lower alpha, which reduces power

Have a limited sample size? You may only be able to detect larger effects

These are not problems—they are the reality of research design.

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
Thinking in tradeoffs
Let’s make this concrete.
Imagine you expect a small effect but can only recruit a small sample.
You now face a decision:

Increase your sample size (if possible)

Accept lower power (risk missing the effect)

Adjust your expectations about detectable effects

Similarly, if you lower alpha to be more conservative:

You reduce false positives

But you also make it harder to detect real effects


Every decision you make in BEAN affects the others.

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
From statistics to research design
Up to this point, much of statistics may have felt like:

calculating values

running tests

interpreting outputs

Power analysis shifts the focus to:

designing studies that are capable of answering your research question.

Instead of asking:

“Is this result significant?”

We start asking:

Was the study capable of detecting the effect?

Was the sample size sufficient?

Were the design choices justified?

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
Final takeaway
If you remember one thing from this chapter, let it be this:

Statistical significance is not just about the data you collect—it is about the decisions you make before you collect the data.

BEAN provides a framework for making those decisions thoughtfully.

title: 8.4 Sample Size and Power Analysis
text:
Looking ahead
In the next chapters, we will return to specific statistical tests. As you learn those tests, keep BEAN in mind:

What effect size is being tested?

What assumptions are being made?

How much power does the study likely have?

These questions will help you move beyond simply running analyses to truly understanding and evaluating research.

title: 9. Inferential Statistics
text:
Types of inferential statistics
In Chapter 7, we introduced the framework for hypothesis testing, and in Chapter 8, we explored the key components that influence our ability to detect effects (BEAN: power, effect size, alpha, and sample size).
At this point, you may be wondering:
That is the focus of this chapter.
There are many different inferential statistical tests, and each is designed to answer a specific type of research question. Choosing the correct test depends on:
Broadly, inferential tests can be grouped into two categories:
These distinctions will become important when deciding which test is appropriate and what to do when assumptions are not met.

title: 9. Inferential Statistics
text:
Parametric statistics: Assume certain characteristics about the data (e.g., normality)

Non-parametric statistics: Make fewer or different assumptions about the data

title: 9. Inferential Statistics
text:
What this chapter will cover
In this chapter, we focus on three key skills:

Choosing the correct inferential test

Based on your research question and variables

Understanding and checking assumptions

What assumptions are

Why they matter

How to evaluate them

Responding to violated assumptions

What to do when assumptions are not met

When to use alternative approaches (e.g., non-parametric tests)

title: 9. Inferential Statistics
text:
Connecting back to the 4-step framework
This chapter builds directly on the 4-step hypothesis testing framework from Chapter 7:

Look at the data

Check assumptions ← major focus of this chapter

Perform the test

Interpret the results


Choosing the correct test and checking assumptions are critical steps that ensure your results are valid and meaningful.

title: 9. Inferential Statistics
text:
Looking ahead
In the following chapters, we will apply these ideas to specific statistical tests (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA, correlation). This chapter provides the foundation you need to make informed decisions about which test to use and whether its results can be trusted.

title: 9.1 Choosing the Correct Test
text:
A decision tool for choosing tests
It is important that you learn how to identify which inferential statistic you should perform. This chart can help you determine what statistical test to perform. Note that on the right dark red boxes are parametric tests, light red boxes are non-parametric tests, and white boxes will not be covered in this class at all (in fact, there are many others not even shown that we won’t cover!). are indicated in either blue (continuous), green (categorical), or teal (both). Number of variables or levels of the variables are either 1 (light orange), 2/2+ (orange), or 3+ (dark orange). Between-subjects designs, meaning designs with different participants in each group, are in black whereas within-subjects designs, meaning designs with the same participants in each group, are in light grey.
It is important that you learn how to identify which inferential statistic you should perform. In this course, we will focus on a structured way of making that decision.
Before looking at specific tests, remember:
The chart below can help you determine which statistical test to perform.
Data types: - Blue = continuous variables
- Green = categorical variables
- Teal = both
Number of variables or levels of the variables: - Light orange = one
- Orange = two
- Dark orange = three or more
Type of design: - Black = between-subjects designs (different participants)
- Light grey = within-subjects designs (same participants)
Another tool that can help you determine the statistical test you want to perform is https://stathand.net/

title: 9.1 Choosing the Correct Test
text:
Dark red boxes = parametric tests

Light red boxes = non-parametric tests

White boxes = not covered in this course

title: 9.1 Choosing the Correct Test
text:
A step-by-step approach
Rather than trying to memorize the chart, use the following process:






Note



Instead of following the chart, you may also find it helpful to first think whether you have a between-subjects or a within-subjects design. If you have groups, are participants in the same conditions (within-subjects) or different conditions (between-subjects)? Then that narrows down the possibility of what inferential statistic you would use. For example, if you know you have a repeated measures design with only one IV, then it’s either a dependent t-test or repeated measures ANOVA depending on whether there are 2 or 3+ categories to the IV.



Step 1: Identify your dependent variable (DV)
In this textbook, we will only be working with one dependent variable.
Ask: - Is your DV categorical (nominal/ordinal)?
- Or continuous (interval/ratio)?






Note



Go back to Chapter 2 “Levels of Measurement” if you need a refresher. This is one of the most important concepts for choosing the correct test.




Step 2: Identify your independent variable(s) (IVs)
Ask: - How many IVs do you have?
- What type are they (categorical or continuous)?
If you have a categorical IV: - How many levels (groups) are there?


Step 3: Determine your design
Ask: - Are the same participants in each condition? → within-subjects
- Are different participants in each condition? → between-subjects






Note



A helpful starting point is to ask:
“Can participants be in more than one condition?”

Yes → within-subjects

No → between-subjects






Step 4: Consider assumptions
For now, assume that assumptions are met. Later in this chapter, we will learn how to check them.
If assumptions are violated, we may need to use a non-parametric alternative.






Note



We will return to assumption checking in Section 9.2.

title: 9.1 Choosing the Correct Test
section: Choosing tests: Forward vs backward mapping
text:
Choosing tests: Forward vs backward mapping
We can use the chart in two ways:

Forward mapping: Start with your data and determine the test

Backward mapping: Start with a test and determine what data it requires


Forward mapping is what you will use most often.

title: 9.1 Choosing the Correct Test
section: Forward mapping: Choose the correct test
text:
Forward mapping: Choose the correct test










Example 1
A researcher is interested in understanding whether athletes have higher English scores than non-athletes.

DV: English test scores → continuous
IVs: One → athletic status
IV type: Categorical
Levels: 2 (athlete vs non-athlete)
Design: Between-subjects (participants cannot be both)
Assumptions: Assume met

Statistical test → Independent t-test

title: 9.1 Choosing the Correct Test
text:
Example 2
A researcher is interested in whether people perform better on exams at the start, middle, or end of the semester. All participants complete all three exams.

DV: Exam score → continuous
IVs: One → time
IV type: Categorical
Levels: 3 (start, middle, end)
Design: Within-subjects (same participants)
Assumptions: Assume met

Statistical test → One-way repeated measures ANOVA

title: 9.1 Choosing the Correct Test
section: Backwards map: Determine the data you need
text:
Backwards map: Determine the data you need
We don’t typically do this in practice, but it helps you understand the tests.
Imagine I gave you a dataset and wanted you to perform two different tests that I told you about. Here are the variables in the dataset:

Mile time (ranging from 5-30 minutes)
BMI (underweight, normal, or overweight)
Happiness at the start of the semester (ranging from 0-10 where 0 is not at all happy and 10 is extremely happy)
Happiness at the end of the semester (ranging from 0-10 where 0 is not at all happy and 10 is extremely happy)

If I told you I wanted you to perform a dependent t-test, what data would you use?

Assuming we meet the assumptions for a parametric test, we need to find a situation in which we have 1 continuous variable and 1 categorical variable with 2 levels in which participants are the same within each category (i.e., within-subjects variable).
We only have three continuous variables: mile time and our two happiness variables.
If we rethink happiness, we can realize that it’s really a within-subjects variable. We are measuring happiness (our continuous DV) across two time points (start and end of the semester).
Therefore, we could perform a dependent t-test with our two happiness data points and see whether happiness differs across time in the semester.

If I told you I wanted you to perform a one-way independent ANOVA, what data would you use?

Assuming we meet the assumptions for a parametric test, we need to find a continuous DV and a single categorical IV with 3 or more levels in which participants are different across categories (i.e., between-subjects design).
We only have three continuous variables: mile time and our two happiness variables.
We only have one categorical variable (BMI), and it has 3 levels: underweight, normal, overweight.
Now this is where we need to think critically. What would be the most interesting test here? How BMI affects happiness or how BMI affects mile time? Weight and performance on running a mile seem to make most sense here. Therefore, we could look at how BMI affects mile time. Though keep in mind we are not randomizing here and so this is not an experimental design!







TipCheck Your Understanding




What is the first thing you should identify when choosing an inferential test?
Why is it important to determine whether your design is within-subjects or between-subjects?
A researcher measures test scores (continuous) for two groups of students (online vs in-person), with different students in each group.
→ What statistical test would you use?
A researcher measures heart rate (continuous) for the same participants before and after exercise.
→ What statistical test would you use?
A researcher measures satisfaction (continuous) across three different teaching methods, with different students in each group.
→ What statistical test would you use?
A researcher measures satisfaction (continuous) across three time points (beginning, middle, end of semester) using the same participants.
→ What statistical test would you use?









TipAnswers






The dependent variable and its level of measurement
Because it determines which statistical tests are appropriate
Independent samples t-test

Continuous DV, 1 categorical IV (2 levels), between-subjects

Dependent (paired) t-test

Continuous DV, 2 levels, within-subjects

One-way independent ANOVA

Continuous DV, 1 categorical IV (3+ levels), between-subjects

One-way repeated measures ANOVA

Continuous DV, 1 categorical IV (3+ levels), within-subjects

title: 9.2 Parametric Assumptions
text:
Why assumptions matter
Most of the inferential statistics we’ll be learning in this class are parametric statistics, which means they rely on certain assumptions about the data.
These assumptions are critical because:
This is why checking assumptions is a key part of Step 2 in our 4-step framework.
In this class, we will focus on assumptions related to the normal distribution and related properties of the data.
Inferential statistics are built on mathematical models. These models assume that the data behave in certain ways. When those assumptions are met, our statistical tests work as expected.
When assumptions are violated:
It’s important to check assumptions because your data may not always be what they seem. You need to look at your data in all sorts of ways to make sure it’s satisfactory for inferential statistics.
There are four basic assumptions of most parametric tests:
This video recaps the rest of the chapter in another format.
Let’s discuss these in turn and how to test for them.

title: 9.2 Parametric Assumptions
text:
p-values may be inaccurate
effect sizes may be misleading
conclusions may not be valid


Checking assumptions helps ensure that your results are trustworthy.




Interval or ratio (i.e., continuous) dependent variable
Independent scores on the dependent variable
Normal distribution
Homogeneity of variances

title: 9.2 Parametric Assumptions
text:
1. Interval/ratio data
If we are performing a parametric test, then the dependent variable (DV) must be measured at the interval or ratio level (i.e., continuous).
It is important that the data have proportional intervals between values. Ordinal variables often do not meet this assumption.
A common issue is treating Likert-scale items as continuous variables. A single Likert item is ordinal, and we technically cannot calculate a mean or difference between ordinal values. However, it is common practice to average or sum multiple Likert items and treat that composite score as continuous (although some argue this is still inappropriate).
There is no formal statistical test for this assumption. Instead, you must determine:

what type of variable you are working with
how it was measured

title: 9.2 Parametric Assumptions
text:
2. Independent scores
This assumption refers to independence between participants, not within participants.

In between-subjects designs, each participant’s score should be independent of every other participant’s score.
In within-subjects designs, scores within a participant are expected to be related—but participants should still be independent of one another.

A violation occurs when data are nested or clustered, such as:

students within the same classroom
employees within the same organization

In these cases, participants within a group may be more similar than expected, violating independence.
Like the previous assumption, this is not something we formally test. Instead, it depends on:

how the data were collected
the structure of the sample

title: 9.2 Parametric Assumptions
text:
3. Normal distribution
For most parametric tests, the dependent variable should be approximately normally distributed.
A normal distribution (or bell-shaped curve) has several key properties:

Data are symmetrically distributed around the mean
Skew and kurtosis are approximately 0
The mean and median are equal
We know the proportion of data within standard deviations of the mean

We will use four complementary approaches to evaluate normality:

Visualize the distribution
Examine skew and kurtosis
Conduct a Shapiro-Wilk test
Examine the Q-Q plot







Note



You should use multiple methods when checking normality. These methods do not always agree, so you must make a judgment as a researcher.
Personally, I tend to prioritize visual inspection and err on the side of caution.


Let’s practice using the variable Height from an example dataset.

a. Visualize the distribution
In jamovi:

Go to Exploration → Descriptives
Under Plots, select:

Histogram and/or density plot
Boxplot, violin plot, and/or data points


We visually inspect whether the distribution resembles a bell-shaped curve.
In the following example, height appears approximately normally distributed.




b. Test the skew and kurtosis
In jamovi:

Go to Exploration → Descriptives → Statistics
Select skew and kurtosis

For our example of height, here is our skew and kurtosis:



Descriptives
Height


Skewness
.230


Std. error skewness
.121


Kurtosis
.113


Std. error kurtosis
.241



We need to calculate z-scores for skew and kurtosis. We do that by dividing the value by its standard error:

Skew: .230 / .121 = 1.90
Kurtosis: .113 / .241 = .47

Interpretation:

If |z| < 1.96 → not significant → approximately normal
If |z| > 1.96 → significant → not normal







Note



Why 1.96? 1.96 is the critical value of z when alpha is .05 and we have a two-tailed test.




c. Shapiro-Wilk test
In jamovi:

Go to Exploration → Descriptives → Statistics
Select Shapiro-Wilk

For our example of height, Shapiro-Wilk’s for height is 68.03, p = .070.
Interpretation:

p > .05 → normality assumption met
p < .05 → violation







Note



This may feel counterintuitive: we want non-significant results because the null hypothesis is that the data are normally distributed.




d. Q-Q plot
In jamovi:

Go to Exploration → Descriptives → Plots
Select Q-Q plot

We look for points falling along the diagonal line.

Left: approximately normal
Right: not normal


Focus primarily on the center of the distribution; small deviations at the tails are common.
Here’s a video by Alexander Swan on interpreting a Q-Q plot in jamovi:

title: 9.2 Parametric Assumptions
text:
4. Homogeneity of variance
The third assumption of many parametric statistics is that the variance in the DV needs to be the same at each level of the IV. If we fail to meet the assumption, we say we have heterogeneity. It might help you to remember that the prefix homo means same and hetero means different.






Note



Normality applies only to the DV overall.
Homogeneity of variance applied to the DV within each group of the IV.


We can evaluate this assumption in three ways:

Visualize inspection
Comapring variance values
Levene’s test







Note



As with normality, these methods may not always agree. Use judgment and justify your decision.



a. Visual inspection
In jamovi:

Go to Exploration and select Descriptives
Move the DV to the variables box
Move the IV to the Split by box
Select boxplot, violin, and/or jittered data

Interpretation: Large differences in the spread of data visually suggest a violation of the assumption of homogeneity of variance
For example, here’s an example of data that violates the assumption of homogeneity of variance (gender by mile time) because the variance in scores for females (coded as 1) is a lot wider than the variance in scores for males (0). I am looking at the data points and violin plot to see the spread; the 1 looks wider where as the 0 looks skinnier.



b. Compare variance values
In jamovi:

Go to Exploration and select Descriptives
Move the DV to the variables box
Move the IV to the Split by box
Under Statistics, select variance

Interpretation: large differences in variance values suggest a violation of the assumption of homogeneity of variance
Example:

Male variance in mile duration in minutes: 6796.20
Female variance in mile duration in minutes: 15401.55

Females have 2.26 times greater variance compared to males. Clearly, there is much greater variability for females than males for time it takes to run the mile.


Levene’s test
In jamovi:

Go to ANOVA → One-way ANOVA
Move your continuous DV to the Dependent Variables box
Move your categorical IV to your Grouping Variable box
Check homogeneity test
Only look at the Levene’s test; ignore everything else at this moment

Interpretation:

p > .05 → assumption met
p < .05 → violation

Here’s our example of gender on mile duration in minutes. In this case, because the p-value is < .05, it indicates we violated the assumption of homogeneity of variance.
Here’s the result of Levene’s test for the effect of gender on mile duration:

Note



Again, we want non-significant results because the null hypothesis is that variances are equal.

title: 9.2 Parametric Assumptions
text:
Recapping parametric assumptions
Let’s summarize the four assumptions and generally how to test for them:

Interval or ratio (i.e., continuous) dependent variable

Know your levels of measurement and make sure the dependent variable is continuous.

Independent scores on the dependent variable

Know your research design and how the data was collected. Make sure that one participant’s data isn’t thought to affect another participant’s data.

Normal distribution of the dependent variable

Visualize the distribution with a histogram and/or density plot of the dependent variable
Test the skew and kurtosis by calculating the z-scores
Test using the Shapiro-Wilk test
Examine the Q-Q plot for deviations from the diagonal line

Homogeneity of variances

Visualize the distribution by a boxplot, violin plot, and/or data (jittered recommended) with the dependent variable split by the independent variable
Examine the variances of the dependent variable split by the independent variable
Test using Levene’s test








TipCheck Your Understanding




Why is it important to check assumptions before running inferential tests?
Which assumptions are determined conceptually (not tested statistically)?
For normality, do we want statistically significant or non-significant results? Why?
What does Levene’s test evaluate?
What does it mean if data are “nested”?









TipAnswers






Because violations can lead to incorrect or misleading conclusions
Continuous DV and independence
Non-significant results, because the null hypothesis is that the data are normally distributed
Whether variances are equal across groups
Data where participants are grouped (e.g., classrooms), making them more similar within groups than expected

title: 9.2 Parametric Assumptions
text:
Looking ahead
In this section, we focused on identifying and evaluating assumptions.
In the next section, we will answer a critical question:

What should we do when these assumptions are violated?

title: 9.3 Violated Assumptions
text:
A general approach
In the previous section, we learned how to identify and evaluate the assumptions of parametric tests.
But what happens when those assumptions are not met?
Instead, they require you to make thoughtful decisions about how to proceed.
When an assumption is violated, you have several options depending on:
Broadly, your options include:
In this section, we will walk through what to do for each assumption. This video also walks through the chapter.

title: 9.3 Violated Assumptions
text:
the type of violation
the severity of the violation
your research goals



choosing a different statistical test
modifying your data (carefully and transparently)
using alternative approaches that are more appropriate for your data

title: 9.3 Violated Assumptions
text:
Interval/ratio data
If you are trying to perform a statistical test with a categorical DV, the answer is simple:

Use a statistical test that is appropriate for a categorical dependent variable.

Do not try to treat a categorical variable as continuous.
For example:

Ordinal DV + categorical IV → chi-square

Ordinal DV + continuous IV → logistic regression (not covered in this course)

Refer back to Section 9.1 to identify appropriate tests for categorical outcomes.

title: 9.3 Violated Assumptions
text:
Independent data
If you violate the assumption of independence, this is a design issue, not a data issue.
This typically occurs with nested or clustered data, such as:

students within classrooms

employees within organizations

In these cases, you need statistical methods that account for dependence, such as:

multilevel modeling (hierarchical modeling)

We will not cover these methods in this course, but it is important to recognize:

Violations of independence cannot be “fixed” with simple data adjustments.

title: 9.3 Violated Assumptions
text:
Normality or homogeneity of variance
If you violate either normality or homogeneity of variance, you have several options:

Remove outliers
Winsorize or trim the data
Transform the data
Perform a non-parametric test







Note



A common concern is whether modifying data “invalidates” the analysis.
Outliers and non-normal data can distort statistical results. Adjusting data is often about making it more appropriate for the assumptions of the test. Any changes should be transparently reported so others can evaluate your decisions.



A: Remove outliers
Before doing anything else, check for outliers.

Univariate outliers → examine individual variables

Multivariate outliers → require more advanced methods (e.g., Mahalanobis distance, Cook’s distance; not covered here)

What can you do with outliers?

Ignore them: not recommended

Delete them: generally not recommended (loss of data)

Winsorize or trim: often appropriate for a few extreme values

Transform the variable: useful when many outliers exist



B. Winsorize or trim the data

Winsorizing → used when both tails have outliers

Trimming → used when outliers are on one side of the distribution of data (e.g., the high or low end but not both)

In both cases, extreme values are replaced with less extreme values.
In jamovi, this can be done using the Transform feature.
Example: - Replace values < 60 with 60 (trimming)
- Replace values > 100 with 100 and values < 20 with 20 (winsorizing)



C. Transforming data
If violations persist, you can transform the entire variable.
Common transformations:










Name
Syntax
Corrects positive skew?
Corrects negative skew?
Helps unequal variances?
